title: panproto: Universal Schema Migration
text:
Preface
You maintain three services. One speaks Protobuf. One uses JSON Schema for validation. The third talks to Bluesky’s AT Protocol. Each time you change a schema, the same question arises: what happens to existing data?
Each ecosystem answers with its own bespoke tooling—Buf for Protobuf, Atlas for SQL, ad-hoc scripts for everything else. But the underlying problem is the same every time. You have a source schema, a target schema, and data that needs to move from one to the other. The only thing that varies across these systems is what “schema” and “data” mean.
panproto is a universal schema migration engine that takes this observation seriously. Instead of hardcoding a particular schema language, it parameterizes over the theory that defines what schemas look like and the theory that defines what data looks like. Everything else—migration validation, data transformation, bidirectional lenses, breaking change detection—is derived from those two theories by the engine.

title: panproto: Universal Schema Migration
text:
What this tutorial covers
This tutorial builds from concrete to abstract and back to concrete:

Part I (Motivation) starts with what you already know: schemas in five formats you use daily. We identify the common structure and name the differences.
Part II (Theory) introduces generalized algebraic theories (GATs)—the mathematical framework that makes the common structure precise. If you’ve defined a TypeScript interface, you already have the intuition; we formalize it.
Part III (Migration) walks through writing, validating, and executing migrations across protocols. You’ll see the data transformation pipeline in detail.
Part IV (Bidirectionality & Safety) covers lenses (every migration runs both ways) and automated breaking change detection.
Part V (Advanced) shows you how to define entirely new schema languages and how panproto can describe—and version—itself.

title: panproto: Universal Schema Migration
text:
Who this is for
You write software that handles structured data. You know what JSON Schema, Protobuf, or SQL DDL look like. You may or may not know category theory—the tutorial introduces the concepts it needs, with references to the literature for deeper exploration.
The appendices include a category theory cheatsheet and a guide to the academic literature for those who want to follow the citations to their source.

title: panproto: Universal Schema Migration
text:
How to use this tutorial
Each chapter builds on the previous ones. Code examples use the panproto TypeScript SDK (@panproto/core) and Rust API (panproto-core). All code in this tutorial is pulled directly from source files using the include-code-files Quarto extension, so what you see here matches the actual implementation.

title: 1  Schemas Everywhere
text:
2 Schemas Everywhere
You already know what a schema is. You write them constantly—every time you define a database table, declare a protobuf message, or sketch out a GraphQL type. Schemas are the skeletons of structured data: they say what fields exist, what types those fields carry, and (sometimes) what constraints hold.
What you might not have noticed is how many schema languages there are, and how eerily similar they look once you squint past the syntax. This chapter makes that observation concrete. We’ll take a single, deliberately boring entity—a blog post—and write its schema in nine different formats drawn from panproto’s coverage of 76 protocols. Then we’ll show how panproto can construct the same schema using its TypeScript SDK, regardless of which protocol you’re targeting.

Every row in that table describes the same structural idea wearing different clothes. A container holds named members. Each member has a type. Some members are required. Some have constraints.
This isn’t a coincidence. These formats all describe the same kind of thing: structured data with named, typed components. The differences are real—SQL has foreign keys, Protobuf has field numbers, GraphQL has nullability as a type feature, XSD has namespace machinery—but the similarities dominate.

title: 1  Schemas Everywhere
1  Schemas Everywhere
text:
2.6 What We’re Looking At
Let’s name what we’ve been seeing, without getting formal yet.
All nine schema formats describe vertices connected by edges:

In JSON Schema, the vertices are types (object, string, array) and the edges are properties linking an object to its field types.
In Protobuf, the vertices are messages and scalar types, and the edges are fields.
In SQL, the vertices are tables and data types, and the edges are columns (linking a table to the type of each column). Foreign keys add edges between tables.
In GraphQL, the vertices are types and the edges are fields.
In ATProto Lexicon, the vertices are records, objects, and primitive types, and the edges are properties.
In Avro, the vertices are records, arrays, and primitive types, and the edges are fields. Logical types annotate a vertex without changing the graph structure.
In TypeScript interfaces, the vertices are interfaces and primitive types, and the edges are members. Optional members (?) carry their own edge annotation.
In OpenAPI, the schema layer is structurally identical to JSON Schema; the additional path layer introduces operation vertices whose edges point into the schema graph via $ref.
In XSD, the vertices are complexTypes, simpleTypes, and built-in primitive types. The edges are element declarations within a sequence, and restrictions on simpleTypes add constraint annotations to edges.

Sometimes the edges carry extra information: a field number in Protobuf, a NOT NULL constraint in SQL, a maxLength in JSON Schema and Lexicon, a minOccurs/maxOccurs pair in XSD. But the fundamental picture is the same: a graph of types connected by typed relationships.
The differences between schema languages come down to:

What kinds of vertices are allowed. SQL has tables and views. Protobuf has messages and enums. GraphQL has types, interfaces, unions, and enums. XSD has complexTypes, simpleTypes, and element declarations as first-class vertices.
What kinds of edges are allowed. SQL columns are different from Protobuf fields, which are different from GraphQL fields, which are different from XSD element declarations within a sequence.
What constraints can be attached. JSON Schema has a rich constraint vocabulary (maxLength, pattern, minimum). Protobuf has almost none built in. SQL constraints are operational (they’re enforced at write time). XSD has a structured restriction vocabulary that can express the same constraints as JSON Schema but requires significantly more nesting to do so.

These are not superficial syntactic differences. They reflect genuine design choices about what each format is optimized for. But they are parametric differences—variations on a common structural theme, not fundamentally different kinds of things.
That structural theme is what panproto is built on. The next chapter gives it a name.

title: 1  Schemas Everywhere
text:
2.7 Looking Ahead
We’ve established that schema languages share a common graph-like structure, and that panproto exploits this commonality. But we’ve been hand-waving about what “graph-like” means. The word “graph” is doing a lot of work, and it turns out that different schema languages need different kinds of graphs.
In Chapter 3, we’ll make this precise. We’ll see that some schemas are plain directed graphs, others are multigraphs, and still others need hypergraphs. These aren’t just academic distinctions—they determine what operations are well-defined on a schema, and they explain why single-protocol tools can’t easily generalize.
But first, take a moment to appreciate the view from here: nine different schema languages, nine different communities, nine different ecosystems—all describing the same blog post, all using the same structural pattern. And these nine are a sample: panproto’s full coverage spans 76 protocols, from serialization formats like MessagePack and CBOR to interface definition languages like Thrift and WSDL to domain-specific schema systems like FHIR and JSON-LD. The fact that a single structural account reaches across all of them is not an accident. It’s a consequence of what schemas are, and understanding that is the goal of this tutorial.

title: 2  The Shape of Schemas
text:
3 The Shape of Schemas
In the previous chapter, we looked at a blog post schema written in five different formats and noticed that they all describe vertices connected by edges. That was a useful intuition, but it was imprecise. The word “graph” can mean many things, and the differences matter.
This chapter makes the graph-theoretic structure of schemas precise. We’ll see that schema languages don’t all use the same kind of graph. Some need multigraphs. Some need hypergraphs. Some need constraints layered on top. Understanding which graph structure a schema language requires is the first step toward understanding what panproto actually parameterizes over.

title: 2  The Shape of Schemas
2  The Shape of Schemas
section: 3.2 Multigraphs: When One Edge Isn’t Enough
text:
3.2 Multigraphs: When One Edge Isn’t Enough
A directed graph has a limitation: between any pair of vertices, there’s at most one edge (in each direction). For many schemas, this is fine. But some schemas need multiple distinct edges between the same pair of vertices.
Consider an ATProto Lexicon record with two string fields:
{
  "type": "object",
  "properties": {
    "displayName": { "type": "string", "maxLength": 640 },
    "description": { "type": "string", "maxLength": 2560 }
  }
}
In graph terms, the object vertex has two edges pointing to the string vertex: one labeled displayName and one labeled description. A plain directed graph can’t represent this—it would collapse the two edges into one. We need a multigraph: a directed graph that allows parallel edges between the same pair of vertices.
A directed multigraph consists of:

A set of vertices
A set of edges, where each edge has a source vertex, a target vertex, and an identity distinct from other edges (even those with the same source and target)

The distinction between a graph and a multigraph might seem pedantic, but it has real consequences for tooling. If your internal representation is a simple adjacency matrix (which can’t represent parallel edges), you’ll silently collapse distinct fields that happen to share a type. panproto uses multigraphs as its baseline representation precisely to avoid this class of bugs.
Here’s what our ATProto profile schema looks like as a multigraph:






graph LR
    Object((object))
    Str((string))

    Object -- "displayName<br/>maxLength: 640" --> Str
    Object -- "description<br/>maxLength: 2560" --> Str




Figure 3.1: An ATProto profile schema as a directed multigraph. The object vertex has two parallel edges to the string vertex, distinguished by their labels (displayName and description).





Both edges connect object to string, but they are distinct: they carry different labels and different constraints. A tool that represents schemas as simple graphs would need some workaround—perhaps making each field its own vertex, or using an adjacency list with labels. A multigraph handles this naturally.

3.2.1 A Richer ATProto Example
Real ATProto schemas are more interesting. Consider a simplified version of the Bluesky post record:






graph LR
    Record((record))
    Str((string))
    Datetime((datetime))
    Array((array))
    Ref((ref))

    Record -- "text" --> Str
    Record -- "createdAt" --> Datetime
    Record -- "tags" --> Array
    Record -- "reply" --> Ref
    Array -- "items" --> Str




Figure 3.2: A simplified Bluesky post schema as a directed multigraph. Multiple edges from the record vertex reflect the multiple properties of the post.





This is still a multigraph—there are no parallel edges in this particular example, but the possibility of parallel edges is what matters. The moment you add a second string field, you need the multigraph structure. Choosing the right graph type up front means you don’t hit a representation barrier as schemas grow more complex.

title: 2  The Shape of Schemas
section: 3.3 Hypergraphs: When Edges Connect More Than Two Things
text:
3.3 Hypergraphs: When Edges Connect More Than Two Things
Directed graphs (and multigraphs) have edges that connect exactly two vertices: a source and a target. But some schema constructs are inherently multi-ary— they involve relationships among three, four, or more entities simultaneously.
The canonical example is a SQL foreign key.
CREATE TABLE posts (
    id          BIGINT GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY,
    text        VARCHAR(3000) NOT NULL,
    author_id   BIGINT NOT NULL,
    created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
);

CREATE TABLE authors (
    id          BIGINT GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY,
    name        VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL
);

ALTER TABLE posts
    ADD CONSTRAINT fk_posts_author
    FOREIGN KEY (author_id) REFERENCES authors (id);
The foreign key fk_posts_author doesn’t connect two things—it connects four:

The source table (posts)
The source column (author_id)
The referenced table (authors)
The referenced column (id)

You can’t faithfully represent this as a binary edge without losing information. If you draw an edge from posts to authors, you’ve lost the column-level detail. If you draw an edge from author_id to id, you’ve lost the table-level context. The foreign key is a single constraint that simultaneously involves all four entities.
This is exactly what hypergraphs are for.
A hypergraph consists of:

A set of vertices
A set of hyperedges, where each hyperedge connects an ordered sequence of vertices (not just two)

A hyperedge can connect any number of vertices—two, three, four, or more. Binary edges are a special case. This makes hypergraphs strictly more expressive than directed graphs or multigraphs.
The theory of hyperedge replacement grammars, developed by (Drewes et al. 1997), provides a formal framework for manipulating hypergraphs with the same rigor that graph grammars provide for ordinary graphs. In this framework, hyperedges can be “replaced” by more complex subhypergraphs according to production rules—a mechanism that maps naturally to schema expansion operations like denormalization or view materialization.
Here’s our SQL schema with its foreign key as a hypergraph:






graph TB
    subgraph Posts ["posts (table)"]
        P_id["id : BIGINT PK"]
        P_text["text : VARCHAR(3000)"]
        P_author["author_id : BIGINT"]
        P_created["created_at : TIMESTAMPTZ"]
    end

    subgraph Authors ["authors (table)"]
        A_id["id : BIGINT PK"]
        A_name["name : VARCHAR(255)"]
    end

    FK{{"fk_posts_author"}}

    FK -. "source table" .-> Posts
    FK -. "source column" .-> P_author
    FK -. "referenced table" .-> Authors
    FK -. "referenced column" .-> A_id

    style FK fill:#f9f,stroke:#333,stroke-dasharray: 5 5




Figure 3.3: A SQL schema with a foreign key represented as a hypergraph. The foreign key constraint (dashed box) connects four entities simultaneously: the source table, source column, referenced table, and referenced column.





The dashed node fk_posts_author is the hyperedge. It has four tentacles, each connecting to a different vertex in the schema. This can’t be reduced to binary edges without either losing information or introducing artificial intermediate vertices.

3.3.1 Why Hyperedges Matter for Tooling
If your schema representation only supports binary edges, you have two options for foreign keys:

Lose information. Draw an edge from posts to authors and hope the column-level detail is recoverable from context. This works until you have composite foreign keys (multiple columns), at which point it breaks.
Introduce intermediate vertices. Create a “foreign key” vertex with binary edges to each of the four participants. This works, but it means your graph has vertices that don’t correspond to anything in the original schema. Every operation (traversal, transformation, serialization) must now handle these phantom vertices correctly.

Hypergraphs avoid both problems. The foreign key is a first-class hyperedge, connecting exactly the vertices it involves. No information lost, no phantoms introduced.
This isn’t just about foreign keys. Composite unique constraints in SQL, oneof declarations in Protobuf, union types in GraphQL—these are all multi-ary relationships that benefit from hypergraph representation.

title: 2  The Shape of Schemas
text:
3.4 Constraints: Decorating the Graph
So far we’ve talked about the shape of the graph: what kinds of vertices and edges exist, how they connect. But schemas carry more information than just connectivity. They have constraints: maxLength, NOT NULL, DEFAULT, pattern, minimum, maxItems, and dozens more.
Constraints don’t change the graph structure—they decorate it. A maxLength constraint on a string property doesn’t add a new vertex or edge; it attaches metadata to an existing edge (the property edge connecting an object to a string type).
We can model this cleanly by distinguishing between:

The underlying graph (vertices and edges/hyperedges), which captures the structural skeleton of the schema
Constraint annotations on vertices and edges, which carry the domain-specific validation rules

This separation is useful because graph-structural operations (like merging two schemas or computing a diff) can often ignore constraints, while constraint-specific operations (like checking whether one schema is a subtype of another) can work with the graph structure as given.

Notice that no schema language we’ve examined requires only a simple directed graph. The moment you allow two fields of the same type (which is… always), you need at least a multigraph. SQL’s and Cassandra’s compound keys push the requirement to hypergraphs. XML/XSD’s element-attribute duality introduces a meta-level that requires annotations beyond ordinary constraints.
These ten protocols are a representative sample. panproto organizes its full set of 76 protocols into 6 theory groups based on the graph structure they require—from Group C (simple multigraphs, e.g. Protobuf, Avro, Thrift) through Group E (multigraphs with constraint and metadata layers, e.g. XML/XSD, HTML). The taxonomy extends uniformly to all 76 protocols; see Chapter 18 for the complete catalog with theory group assignments.
This taxonomy explains a pattern you’ve probably noticed in the ecosystem: schema tools tend to be protocol-specific. Buf is excellent for Protobuf. Atlas is excellent for SQL. GraphQL Code Generator is excellent for GraphQL. But none of them generalize across protocols.

title: 2  The Shape of Schemas
text:
3.6 Why Single-Protocol Tools Can’t Generalize
The reason is structural, not incidental. Each tool hardcodes a specific graph type:

Buf assumes a multigraph structure (messages with fields). It understands Protobuf’s field numbering, package namespaces, and options. But it has no representation for hyperedges, so it can’t natively express SQL foreign keys.
Atlas assumes a hypergraph structure (tables with columns and multi-ary constraints). It understands SQL’s type system, migration semantics, and constraint checking. But its vertex types are SQL-specific (tables, views, indexes), not Protobuf-specific (messages, enums, services).
GraphQL Code Generator assumes a multigraph with GraphQL’s specific vertex types (Object, Interface, Union, Enum, Scalar). It can’t represent Protobuf’s field numbering or SQL’s CHECK constraints.

Each tool is a perfect fit for its target protocol and a poor fit for everything else. This isn’t a failure of engineering—it’s a consequence of fixing the graph structure at design time.
panproto takes a different approach: it parameterizes over the graph structure. Instead of hardcoding “the schema is a multigraph with these specific vertex and edge types,” panproto lets you specify what kinds of vertices, edges, and constraints your schema language needs. The same core algorithms—diffing, merging, migration planning, code generation—work across any graph structure that fits the framework.
This parameterization is not free. It requires a mathematical framework expressive enough to describe multigraphs, hypergraphs, and constrained variants in a uniform way. That framework exists: it comes from the categorical treatment of schemas developed by (Spivak 2012), who showed that database schemas can be viewed as categories (with tables as objects and foreign keys as morphisms) and that schema mappings are functors between those categories. panproto generalizes this functorial perspective beyond relational databases to arbitrary schema languages.
The key insight from Spivak’s work is that once you view schemas as categorical structures, you get a rich toolkit for free: limits and colimits give you schema merging and intersection, functors give you schema mappings and translations, and natural transformations give you data migration along those mappings. These aren’t metaphors—they’re precise mathematical constructions with well-defined properties.






NoteThe Parameterization Principle



Single-protocol tools fix the graph structure and optimize for a specific schema language. panproto parameterizes the graph structure and provides uniform algorithms that work across schema languages. This is the fundamental architectural difference—and it’s what makes cross-protocol schema operations possible.

title: 2  The Shape of Schemas
text:
3.7 From Graphs to Theories
We’ve established that schema languages have graph-theoretic structure, and that different languages need different kinds of graphs. But we’ve been describing these graph structures informally—listing their components (vertices, edges, operations) in prose.
There’s a precise way to describe a graph structure: you specify its sorts (the kinds of things that exist), its operations (the functions between those sorts), and its equations (the laws those operations obey). A directed graph, for instance, has two sorts (Vertex and Edge), two operations (src and tgt, both from Edge to Vertex), and no equations. A multigraph has the same description—the “multi” comes from not requiring edges to be uniquely determined by their source and target.
This sort-operation-equation description is called a theory, and the things that satisfy a theory are called models. A specific ATProto schema is a model of the multigraph theory. A specific SQL schema is a model of the hypergraph-with-constraints theory.
If the language of sorts, operations, and equations sounds suspiciously like an abstract algebra course, that’s because it is. The next chapter introduces the specific algebraic framework panproto uses—Generalized Algebraic Theories—and shows how the Rust implementation represents theories and models as concrete data structures. The math will earn its keep: it’s what makes panproto’s parameterization possible.

title: 2  The Shape of Schemas
text:
3.8 Summary
This chapter established three ideas:

Schema languages have graph-theoretic structure. Types are vertices; fields, columns, and properties are edges. This is not a metaphor—it’s a precise structural description.
Different schema languages need different kinds of graphs. Multigraphs for parallel fields (most languages). Hypergraphs for multi-ary constraints (SQL). Constraints as annotations on any of these.
Single-protocol tools hardcode a specific graph structure. This is why Buf can’t do SQL migrations and Atlas can’t lint Protobuf. panproto parameterizes over the graph structure instead.

The question now is: how do you precisely describe a graph structure so that software can work with it generically? That’s the subject of the next chapter.






Drewes, Frank, Hans-Jörg Kreowski, and Annegret Habel. 1997. “Hyperedge Replacement Graph Grammars.” In Handbook of Graph Grammars and Computing by Graph Transformation, edited by Gert Rozenberg, vol. 1, 1. World Scientific. https://doi.org/10.1142/9789812384720_0002.


Ehrig, Hartmut, Karsten Ehrig, Ulrike Prange, and Gabriele Taentzer. 2006. Fundamentals of Algebraic Graph Transformation. Monographs in Theoretical Computer Science. Springer. https://doi.org/10.1007/3-540-31188-2.


Ehrig, Hartmut, Michael Pfender, and Hans Jürgen Schneider. 1973. “Graph-Grammars: An Algebraic Approach.” 14th Annual IEEE Symposium on Switching and Automata Theory (FOCS 1973), 167–80. https://doi.org/10.1109/SWAT.1973.11.


Spivak, David I. 2012. “Functorial Data Migration.” Information and Computation 217: 31–51. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ic.2012.05.001.

title: 3  What Is a Theory?
text:
4 What Is a Theory?
The previous chapter ended with a promissory note: we’d find a precise way to describe graph structures so that panproto can parameterize over them. This chapter delivers on that promise by introducing Generalized Algebraic Theories (GATs)—the mathematical framework at the heart of panproto’s design.
GATs might sound intimidating, but the core idea is one you already use every day. If you’ve ever written a TypeScript interface and then implemented it with a class, you’ve already grasped the essential pattern. A GAT theory is an interface; a model is an implementation. The rest is details—important details, but details that build on intuition you already have.

title: 3  What Is a Theory?
3  What Is a Theory?
section: 4.2 Theory of a Monoid: The Simplest Nontrivial Example

title: 3  What Is a Theory?
section: 4.3 Theory of a Graph: Where Schemas Enter
text:
4.3 Theory of a Graph: Where Schemas Enter
Now let’s look at a more relevant theory: the theory of a directed graph.
Sorts:

Vertex — the vertices (nodes)
Edge — the edges (arrows)

Operations:

src(e: Edge) -> Vertex — the source of an edge
tgt(e: Edge) -> Vertex — the target of an edge

Equations:

(none)

That’s it. A directed graph is a pair of sets (vertices and edges) with two functions (source and target) and no equations. The absence of equations is significant: it means there are no laws governing how src and tgt relate to each other. Any assignment of source and target vertices to edges is valid.
A model of this theory is a specific graph. An ATProto schema, viewed as a multigraph (from the previous chapter), is a model:

Vertex = the set of type nodes (object, string, datetime, …)
Edge = the set of property edges (text, createdAt, tags, …)
src(text) = object, tgt(text) = string
src(createdAt) = object, tgt(createdAt) = datetime
src(tags) = object, tgt(tags) = array

The schema is the model. The theory of a graph describes the structural shape that ATProto schemas, JSON Schemas, GraphQL type systems, and Protobuf message definitions all share (at their simplest).

4.3.1 Sorts in Rust
In panproto-gat, a sort is a named entity with optional parameters (we’ll get to parameters shortly):
#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, Hash, serde::Serialize, serde::Deserialize)]
pub struct Sort {
    /// The sort name (e.g., "Vertex", "Edge", "Hom").
    pub name: String,
    /// Parameters this sort depends on. Empty for simple sorts.
    pub params: Vec<SortParam>,
}
A Sort has a name and a list of parameters. For the graph theory, we’d have two sorts—Vertex and Edge—each with an empty parameter list. The parameter list becomes important when we introduce dependent sorts later in this chapter.


4.3.2 Operations in Rust
An operation connects input sorts to an output sort:
#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, Hash, serde::Serialize, serde::Deserialize)]
pub struct Operation {
    /// The operation name (e.g., "src", "tgt", "compose").
    pub name: String,
    /// Typed inputs as `(param_name, sort_name)` pairs.
    pub inputs: Vec<(String, String)>,
    /// The output sort name.
    pub output: String,
}
An Operation has a name, a list of input sorts (its parameters), and an output sort (its return type). The src operation of the graph theory has one input (of sort Edge) and returns sort Vertex. Similarly for tgt.
This is directly analogous to a function signature in any typed language. The operation src: Edge -> Vertex is the theory’s version of a method src(e: Edge): Vertex.


4.3.3 Theories in Rust
A theory collects sorts, operations, and equations into a single structure:
#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, serde::Serialize, serde::Deserialize)]
pub struct Theory {
    /// The theory name (e.g., "Monoid", "Category").
    pub name: String,
    /// Names of parent theories this theory extends.
    pub extends: Vec<String>,
    /// Sort declarations.
    pub sorts: Vec<Sort>,
    /// Operation declarations.
    pub ops: Vec<Operation>,
    /// Equations (axioms).
    pub eqs: Vec<Equation>,
}
The Theory struct holds vectors of sorts, operations, and equations. It also has a name and optional metadata. This is the central data structure that panproto’s algorithms operate on: when you ask panproto to diff two schemas, or generate code from a schema, or plan a migration, it first consults the theory to understand what structural shape it’s working with.

title: 3  What Is a Theory?
text:
4.4 From Algebraic Theories to GATs
So far, we’ve seen theories where sorts are simple named entities: Carrier, Vertex, Edge. These are ordinary algebraic theories, the kind studied by Lawvere (1963) in his foundational work on functorial semantics. Lawvere showed that algebraic theories (like the theory of a monoid, or the theory of a group) can be understood as categories with specific structure, and that models are product-preserving functors from the theory category to the category of sets.
This functorial perspective is powerful—it gives you a precise notion of “structure-preserving map” between models (natural transformations between functors) and lets you use categorical machinery to construct new models from old ones. But ordinary algebraic theories have a limitation: all sorts are independent.
In the theory of a graph, Vertex and Edge don’t depend on each other. You declare them separately and then connect them via operations. This is fine for graphs, but it’s not enough for the richer structures that schema languages actually need.
Consider constraints. In the previous chapter, we noted that constraints like maxLength and NOT NULL are annotations on edges or vertices. But which constraints are valid depends on which vertex or edge you’re annotating:

A maxLength constraint makes sense on a string-typed property edge, but not on a foreign key.
A CHECK constraint makes sense on a SQL column, but not on a Protobuf field number.
A maxGraphemes constraint makes sense on an ATProto string property, but not on an integer property.

The sort of “valid constraints” depends on which vertex (or edge) you’re looking at. This is a dependent sort—a sort parameterized by a term of another sort. Ordinary algebraic theories can’t express this. GATs can.

title: 3  What Is a Theory?
text:
4.5 Dependent Sorts: The Key Generalization
A Generalized Algebraic Theory (GAT) extends ordinary algebraic theories by allowing sorts to depend on terms of other sorts. The foundational treatment is due to Cartmell (1986), who introduced GATs as a framework for dependent type theories—type systems where the type of a thing can depend on the value of another thing.
In concrete terms, a dependent sort looks like this:

Vertex — an ordinary sort (no dependencies)
Edge — an ordinary sort
Constraint(v: Vertex) — a dependent sort, parameterized by a vertex

The sort Constraint(v: Vertex) is a family of sorts, one for each vertex. Constraint(stringVertex) is the sort of constraints that apply to string types. Constraint(intVertex) is the sort of constraints that apply to integer types. These might be completely different sets: string constraints include maxLength and pattern, while integer constraints include minimum and maximum.
This is exactly the expressiveness we need for schema languages. The “constraint vocabulary” of a schema language isn’t a flat set—it’s a vertex-indexed family. GATs let us express this directly.

4.5.1 SortParam in Rust
panproto-gat represents sort parameters—the mechanism for dependent sorts—with a dedicated type:
#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, Hash, serde::Serialize, serde::Deserialize)]
pub struct SortParam {
    /// The parameter name (e.g., "a", "b").
    pub name: String,
    /// The sort this parameter ranges over (e.g., "Ob").
    pub sort: String,
}
A SortParam captures the name and sort of each parameter in a dependent sort. For Constraint(v: Vertex), there would be one SortParam with name "v" and sort reference pointing to the Vertex sort. The Sort struct we saw earlier holds a Vec<SortParam>, which is empty for ordinary sorts and non-empty for dependent sorts.


4.5.2 Building the Schema Theory
With dependent sorts, we can describe the full structure of a schema language as a GAT. Here’s a sketch of what the “theory of an ATProto schema” looks like:
Sorts:

TypeNode — the types in a schema (object, string, array, ref, …)
PropEdge — the property edges connecting types
Constraint(t: TypeNode) — constraints that apply to a specific type node

Operations:

src(p: PropEdge) -> TypeNode — source of a property edge
tgt(p: PropEdge) -> TypeNode — target of a property edge
required(p: PropEdge) -> Bool — whether a property is required
maxLength(c: Constraint(stringNode)) -> Nat — the max length constraint (only defined for string-typed nodes)

Equations:

(domain-specific laws about constraint well-formedness)

The dependent sort Constraint(t: TypeNode) is doing the heavy lifting here. It lets us express that maxLength is only a valid constraint on string nodes—not as a runtime check, but as a structural property of the theory itself. A model that tried to attach a maxLength to an integer node wouldn’t be a valid model, because the types wouldn’t match.


4.5.3 Why Not Just Use Ordinary Sorts?
You could represent constraints without dependent sorts. One approach: have a flat Constraint sort and an operation appliesTo(c: Constraint) -> TypeNode, then check at runtime that constraints are only applied to appropriate nodes.
This works, but it pushes a structural invariant into runtime validation. The theory itself doesn’t prevent ill-formed models—you need additional code to reject them. With dependent sorts, the invariant is part of the theory. The type system enforces it.
This is the same tradeoff you face when choosing between any and a precise type in TypeScript. Both “work” in the sense that correct programs can be written with either. But precise types catch more errors at compile time, make the code more self-documenting, and enable better tooling. Dependent sorts do the same for theories.

title: 3  What Is a Theory?
text:
4.6 The GAT-EAT Equivalence
There’s a subtlety worth addressing. GATs with dependent sorts are expressive, but they’re also complex—implementing a full dependent type checker is a significant engineering effort. panproto avoids this by leveraging a classical result from categorical logic: under suitable conditions, GATs are equivalent to Essentially Algebraic Theories (EATs).
The key reference here is (Adámek and Rosický 1994), which establishes the theory of locally presentable categories. In this framework, the categories of models of GATs and EATs coincide for a broad class of theories—precisely the class that arises in practice for schema descriptions. The practical consequence: panproto can implement dependent sorts using partial operations (operations that are only defined when a condition holds) rather than a full dependent type system.
For the Constraint(t: TypeNode) example, the EAT version would have:

An ordinary sort Constraint (not dependent)
A total operation appliesTo(c: Constraint) -> TypeNode
A partial operation maxLength(c: Constraint) -> Nat, defined only when appliesTo(c) = stringNode

The partiality encodes the dependency. Mathematically, this gives the same category of models. Computationally, it’s much simpler to implement—you need partial functions, not dependent types.
This is an architectural choice inherited from GATlab (Lynch et al. 2024), a Julia framework for computational category theory that panproto adapts to Rust. GATlab demonstrated that the GAT-EAT equivalence is practical, not just theoretical: real software can exploit it to get the expressiveness of dependent sorts without the complexity of dependent type checking.

title: 3  What Is a Theory?
text:
4.7 Theories for Schema Languages
Let’s put this all together by sketching the theories for the schema languages from the previous chapters.

4.7.1 Theory of JSON Schema
Sorts:

SchemaNode — schema objects (the things that have a type keyword)
Property — named properties of object schemas
Constraint(s: SchemaNode) — constraints applicable to a schema node

Operations:

owner(p: Property) -> SchemaNode — the object that owns this property
target(p: Property) -> SchemaNode — the type of this property
name(p: Property) -> String — the property name
required(p: Property) -> Bool — whether the property is required
schemaType(s: SchemaNode) -> TypeTag — the type keyword (object, string, array, …)
maxLength(c: Constraint(stringNode)) -> Nat
pattern(c: Constraint(stringNode)) -> Regex
minimum(c: Constraint(numericNode)) -> Number

Equations:

owner(p) is always of type object (properties belong to objects)



4.7.2 Theory of SQL DDL
Sorts:

Table — database tables
Column — columns in tables
ForeignKey — foreign key constraints (a hyperedge sort)
Constraint(c: Column) — column-level constraints

Operations:

table(c: Column) -> Table — the table that owns this column
colType(c: Column) -> DataType — the data type of a column
srcTable(fk: ForeignKey) -> Table
srcColumn(fk: ForeignKey) -> Column
refTable(fk: ForeignKey) -> Table
refColumn(fk: ForeignKey) -> Column
notNull(c: Column) -> Bool
defaultVal(c: Column) -> Option<Expr>

Equations:

table(srcColumn(fk)) = srcTable(fk) — the source column belongs to the source table
table(refColumn(fk)) = refTable(fk) — the referenced column belongs to the referenced table

Notice how the equations for SQL DDL express structural invariants about foreign keys: the source column must actually be in the source table. These equations are not arbitrary—they’re the well-formedness conditions that every SQL database enforces. By making them part of the theory, panproto can verify schema well-formedness before generating any SQL.


4.7.3 Theory of Protobuf
Sorts:

Message — protobuf messages
Field — fields within messages
EnumType — enum declarations
EnumValue — values within enums

Operations:

message(f: Field) -> Message — the message that owns this field
fieldType(f: Field) -> TypeRef — the type of a field
fieldNumber(f: Field) -> Nat — the wire format number
enumType(v: EnumValue) -> EnumType — the enum that owns this value
enumNumber(v: EnumValue) -> Int — the numeric value

Equations:

For distinct fields f1, f2 in the same message: fieldNumber(f1) != fieldNumber(f2) — field numbers are unique within a message

The uniqueness equation is what makes Protobuf’s wire format work: field numbers must be unique so that the binary encoder can unambiguously identify fields. This is a Protobuf-specific structural law, not shared by JSON Schema or SQL.

title: 3  What Is a Theory?
text:
4.8 The Functorial View
We’ve been building up a picture: theories describe shapes, models are things with those shapes, and different schema languages correspond to models of different theories. There’s one more piece to the picture: morphisms between models.
Given two models of the same theory—say, two ATProto schemas—a morphism between them is a structure-preserving map. It sends vertices to vertices, edges to edges, and respects the src and tgt operations. In schema terms, a morphism is a schema mapping: a systematic translation from one schema to another that preserves structural relationships.
This is the functorial perspective that Spivak (2012) developed for database schemas and that panproto generalizes. In Spivak’s framework:

A schema is a category (a model of the theory of categories)
A schema mapping is a functor between schemas
An instance (data conforming to a schema) is a functor from the schema to the category of sets
Data migration along a schema mapping is a composition of functors

panproto works at one level of abstraction higher: instead of fixing the theory to be “the theory of categories” (which suffices for relational databases), it parameterizes over the theory. This lets it handle schema languages that aren’t naturally categorical—like Protobuf, where field numbers are an essential part of the structure that categories don’t capture.
The general picture, combining the categorical perspective with GATs, is:

You choose a theory (e.g., the theory of SQL schemas, the theory of Protobuf schemas)
A specific schema is a model of that theory
A schema mapping is a morphism of models
panproto’s algorithms work at the level of theories and models, so they apply uniformly across schema languages

This is what it means for panproto to “parameterize over schema structure.” The theory is the parameter. Change the theory, and you get a different schema language—but the same algorithms.

title: 3  What Is a Theory?
text:
4.9 The Category Theory Landscape
For readers who want to go deeper, here’s how the references connect.
The general framework of category theory, as presented in (Mac Lane 1998), provides the ambient mathematical universe. Categories, functors, natural transformations, limits, and colimits are the basic vocabulary. For a gentler introduction oriented toward computer science, (Barr and Wells 1990) covers the same material with more attention to computational applications.
Within this universe, Lawvere (1963) identified that algebraic theories (like the theory of monoids or groups) are themselves categories with specific structure, and that models are product-preserving functors from the theory category to the category of sets. This was a revolutionary insight: it turned “satisfying axioms” into “preserving structure,” which is something categories are designed to talk about.
Cartmell (1986) generalized Lawvere’s framework to handle dependent sorts, arriving at GATs. This is the level of generality panproto needs: schema languages have dependent structure (constraints depend on types), and ordinary algebraic theories can’t capture that.
(Adámek and Rosický 1994) provided the theoretical bridge between GATs and EATs through the theory of locally presentable categories. This result is what lets panproto implement dependent sorts without a full dependent type checker—a crucial engineering simplification.
Lynch et al. (2024) demonstrated that this entire framework can be implemented practically in a computational system (GATlab, in Julia). panproto adapts GATlab’s architecture to Rust, gaining static typing, zero-cost abstractions, and Wasm compilation while retaining the mathematical foundations.






NoteThe Key Insight



A theory is a description of a shape. A model is a thing that has that shape.
JSON Schema, Protobuf, SQL DDL, GraphQL SDL, ATProto Lexicon—these are all models of different theories. The theories differ (multigraphs vs. hypergraphs, different constraint sorts), but they’re all expressed in the same meta-framework: GATs.
panproto is parameterized by the theory. That’s how it handles multiple schema languages with uniform algorithms. You don’t write a schema differ for Protobuf and a separate one for SQL. You write a schema differ that works for any theory, and then you instantiate it with the specific theory you need.

title: 3  What Is a Theory?
text:
4.10 What We’ve Built So Far
Let’s take stock. Over three chapters, we’ve established:

Schemas are everywhere (Chapter 1). Different schema languages describe the same structural idea—types connected by typed relationships—in different syntaxes.
Schemas have graph-theoretic structure (Chapter 2). The specific kind of graph varies: multigraphs for most languages, hypergraphs for SQL. Constraints decorate the graph structure.
Graph structures are described by theories (this chapter). A theory specifies sorts, operations, and equations. A model is a concrete thing satisfying the theory. Dependent sorts (GATs) let theories express type-indexed constraints.

Together, these three ideas form the conceptual foundation of panproto. The next chapters will build on this foundation, moving from the “what” to the “how”: how theories are implemented in Rust, how models are constructed and manipulated, and how the TypeScript SDK exposes all of this to application developers.

title: 3  What Is a Theory?
text:
4.11 Summary

A theory consists of sorts, operations, and equations. It describes a shape without committing to a specific instance.
A model assigns concrete sets and functions to a theory’s sorts and operations, satisfying its equations. A model is a thing that has the shape the theory describes.
Ordinary algebraic theories (Lawvere 1963) handle independent sorts: Carrier, Vertex, Edge.
Generalized Algebraic Theories (Cartmell 1986) handle dependent sorts: Constraint(v: Vertex), where the sort of constraints depends on which vertex you’re looking at. This is the expressiveness panproto needs for schema constraints.
The GAT-EAT equivalence (Adámek and Rosický 1994) lets panproto implement dependent sorts using partial operations rather than a full dependent type system.
GATlab (Lynch et al. 2024) demonstrated this architecture computationally in Julia. panproto adapts it to Rust.
Schema languages are models of different theories. panproto parameterizes over the theory, enabling uniform algorithms across schema languages.

Barr, Michael, and Charles Wells. 1990. Category Theory for Computing Science. Prentice Hall.


Cartmell, John. 1986. “Generalised Algebraic Theories and Contextual Categories.” Annals of Pure and Applied Logic 32: 209–43. https://doi.org/10.1016/0168-0072(86)90053-9.

title: 4  Protocols as Parameters
text:
4.1 The Two-Parameter Architecture
In the previous chapter, we saw that a theory defines an interface: a collection of sorts, operations, and axioms that any conforming model must satisfy. Theories let us talk about “categories,” “graphs,” “constraints,” and so on without committing to a particular representation. In this chapter, we put that machinery to work by asking a sharper question: what is a protocol?
The answer in panproto is surprisingly concise. A protocol is a pair of theories—one controlling the shape of schemas, and one controlling the shape of data. Everything else—migration, diffing, breaking-change detection, bidirectional lenses—is derived from that pair by mechanical reasoning. You do not write protocol-specific logic. You declare two theories and let the engine do the rest.
panproto currently supports 76 protocols organized into six theory groups, each defined by a characteristic schema theory and instance theory combination.
Every protocol that panproto supports is described by exactly two parameters:
The following table catalogues all 76 protocols and their decomposition into this two-parameter form. The protocols are grouped by their shared (schema theory, instance theory) signature; within each group every row is an instance of the same theoretical machinery. Each row is a protocol fully characterized by its schema theory, its instance theory, and a restriction operator that implements migration.
A few things to notice:

Instance theory: the algebraic theory whose models are data instances conforming to a schema. This determines what “a record” or “a row” looks like once you have a schema in hand.
