The schema theory is assembled by taking a colimit (a structured union) of simpler component theories. This is exactly the colimit construction from Chapter 3.
Instance theories cluster into three families. There are functor-based instances (ThFunctor), tree-based instances (ThWType), and flat instances (ThFlat). This is the deepest structural split in the table, and it determines how migration works at the data level. ThFlat is best understood as a degenerate case of ThWType restricted to depth-1 trees.
The restriction operator is determined by the instance theory. Functor instances always migrate via precomposition (\(\Delta_F\)). W-type instances always migrate via the signature-restriction pipeline. Flat instances use the simpler field-projection operator, which is the depth-1 specialization of tree surgery. You don’t choose a migration strategy; it follows from the theory.

title: 4  Protocols as Parameters
text:
4.2 Three Families of Instances
The instance theory is where the rubber meets the road. It determines what “data” means for a given schema, and therefore what “migrating data” means. Let’s look at the three families in detail.

4.2.1 Set-Valued Functors
The functor model comes from the categorical databases tradition, developed most thoroughly by Spivak (2012) and formalized in the CQL system by Schultz et al. (2017).
The idea is direct. A schema is a category \(\mathcal{C}\)—objects are tables (or types), morphisms are foreign keys (or relationships). An instance of that schema is a functor \(I : \mathcal{C} \to \mathbf{Set}\). In concrete terms:

Each table \(T\) in the schema maps to a set \(I(T)\)—the rows of that table.
Each foreign key \(f : T_1 \to T_2\) maps to a function \(I(f) : I(T_1) \to I(T_2)\)—the lookup that sends each row of \(T_1\) to its corresponding row in \(T_2\).

This is a faithful model of relational data. The functoriality condition (\(I(g \circ f) = I(g) \circ I(f)\)) ensures that following two foreign keys in sequence gives the same result whether you compose them first or chase them one at a time. Referential integrity is built into the mathematics.
Migration under the functor model is precomposition. Given a schema morphism \(F : \mathcal{C}_1 \to \mathcal{C}_2\) (a functor mapping the old schema into the new one), and an instance \(I : \mathcal{C}_2 \to \mathbf{Set}\) living over the new schema, you get an instance \(\Delta_F(I) = I \circ F : \mathcal{C}_1 \to \mathbf{Set}\) over the old schema. In operational terms: for each old table \(T\), you look up which new table \(F(T)\) it maps to, and pull back that table’s rows. Foreign keys compose automatically.
This is the pullback migration functor \(\Delta_F\) from (Spivak 2012). It has left and right adjoints (\(\Sigma_F\) and \(\Pi_F\)) that handle the “push data forward” direction, but for now, \(\Delta_F\) is the one that matters for understanding the restriction column of the protocol table.


4.2.2 W-Types: Tree-Shaped Data
The functor model works beautifully for relational data, but most modern protocols—ATProto, GraphQL, JSON Schema, TypeScript, and the majority of the 76 protocols panproto supports—traffic in tree-shaped data. A Bluesky post is not a row in a table; it is a JSON document with nested objects, arrays, and references. The right mathematical model for this kind of data is a W-type (wellfounded tree), studied by Gambino and Hyland (2004) in the context of dependent type theory and related to the containers of Abbott et al. (2005).
A W-type instance over a schema is a finitely-branching tree where:

Each node is anchored to a schema vertex. The root might be anchored to a Post vertex, a child to a Text vertex, a sibling to a CreatedAt vertex.
Each branch (parent-to-child edge) is labeled by a schema edge. The edge from Post to Text might be labeled text, the edge from Post to CreatedAt might be labeled createdAt.
The tree is wellfounded. No infinite descending chains. Every path from root to leaf is finite.

This is exactly the shape of a JSON document, an ATProto record, or a GraphQL response. The schema tells you which node types exist and which edges connect them. The instance is a particular tree that conforms to those constraints.
Migration under the W-type model is more involved than precomposition, because you can’t just “pull back” a tree through a functor. Instead, the engine runs a multi-step pipeline:

Signature restriction: remove nodes anchored to vertices not in the image of the migration map.
Reachability BFS: starting from the root, find all nodes still reachable after the pruning.
Ancestor contraction: when intermediate nodes are removed, reconnect their children to the nearest surviving ancestor.
Edge resolution: relabel branches according to the migration’s edge map, resolving ambiguities where necessary.
Fan reconstruction: rebuild the branching structure of the tree to maintain wellfoundedness.

We will walk through this pipeline concretely in Chapter 7. For now, the key point is that this pipeline is derived from the theory pair. The engine knows it needs to do tree surgery (rather than precomposition) because the instance theory is ThWType rather than ThFunctor.


4.2.3 Flat Instances: Depth-1 Trees
ThFlat is not an independent theory so much as a disciplined restriction of ThWType. When the schema is a simple graph (no parallel edges, no recursive types) and instances are constrained to trees of depth at most 1, the full W-type machinery reduces to a much simpler operation: field projection.
A flat instance over a simple-graph schema is a collection of key-value records where each key is a schema edge and each value is a leaf datum. Because the tree never nests beyond depth 1, contraction never fires, reachability is trivial, and edge resolution degenerates to a straightforward lookup. The restriction operator collapses to: keep the fields whose schema edges appear in the image of the migration map; discard the rest.
This simplification is not merely computational. ThFlat captures a genuine semantic boundary: protocols like Protobuf, Avro, and Thrift are deliberately flat. They are designed for high-throughput serialization where recursive nesting would impose unacceptable overhead. Treating ThFlat as a proper theory (rather than a naming convention) ensures that panproto can enforce the depth-1 constraint at the theory level and reject schemas that would violate it.
The relationship is:
\[\text{ThFlat} \hookrightarrow \text{ThWType}\]
where the inclusion is a theory morphism that restricts the tree-height axioms. A ThFlat instance is a ThWType instance; the converse does not hold.

title: 4  Protocols as Parameters
text:
4.3 Comparing the Three Models
It’s worth pausing to see these three instance theories side by side, because the choice among them is the central architectural decision that distinguishes protocol families.



Table 4.2: Comparing the three instance theory families.











Aspect
Functor (ThFunctor)
W-Type (ThWType)
Flat (ThFlat)




Data shape
Flat tables of rows
Nested trees of nodes
Depth-1 key-value records


Schema role
Category (objects + morphisms)
Graph (vertices + edges)
Simple graph (no parallel edges)


Instance role
Functor to Set
Wellfounded tree
Depth-1 tree


Migration
Precomposition (\(\Delta_F\))
Tree surgery pipeline
Field projection


Canonical users
SQL, CQL, Parquet
ATProto, GraphQL, TypeScript
Protobuf, Avro, Thrift


Compositionality
Functorial (automatic)
Requires reachability analysis
Trivial (projection)


Adjoints available
Yes (\(\Sigma_F\), \(\Pi_F\))
Not in general
Not in general


Relationship
Independent
Generalizes ThFlat
Special case of ThWType






None of these models is “better” in the abstract. The functor model has superior compositional properties—migration functors compose, and the adjoint triple gives you round-tripping for free. But the functor model demands that your data is tabular, which is a poor fit for document-oriented protocols. The W-type model handles trees natively, at the cost of more complex migration logic and fewer automatic guarantees. ThFlat threads a middle path: it gives up the generality of arbitrary nesting in exchange for a migration operator so simple it is nearly free to compute.
panproto’s contribution is that you don’t have to choose one model for all protocols. Each protocol declares its own instance theory, and the engine adapts accordingly. SQL gets precomposition. ATProto gets tree surgery. Protobuf gets field projection. The outer framework—schema morphisms, existence checks, breaking-change detection—works uniformly across all three.

title: 4  Protocols as Parameters
4  Protocols as Parameters
text:
4.5 Why Colimits for Schema Theories?
You may have noticed that most schema theories in Table 4.1 are colimits of simpler theories. This is not an accident. Real-world protocols are assembled from orthogonal concerns:

Graph structure: vertices and directed edges (ThGraph).
Constraint structure: invariants on vertex/edge data (ThConstraint).
Multiplicity structure: arrays, optional fields, repeated edges (ThMulti).
Interface structure: abstract types implemented by concrete ones (ThInterface).
Hyperedge structure: edges connecting more than two vertices simultaneously (ThHyperEdge).
Metadata structure: out-of-band annotation layers attached to the schema or instance (ThMeta).

These concerns are largely independent of one another. A protocol might need graphs and constraints but not interfaces (ATProto). Another might need graphs, constraints, multiplicity, and interfaces (GraphQL). Still another might need the full multigraph combination plus a metadata layer, as in the tree-plus-metadata protocols of Group E. The colimit construction lets you assemble exactly the combination you need, with the categorical machinery ensuring that the pieces fit together coherently.
For example, ATProto’s schema theory is:
\[\text{ThATProtoSchema} = \text{colim}(\text{ThGraph}, \text{ThConstraint}, \text{ThMulti})\]
This says: an ATProto schema is a structure that is simultaneously a graph, a constrained structure, and a multiplicity-aware structure, with the overlapping parts (the shared sorts and operations) identified. The colimit handles the identification automatically.
SQL’s schema theory replaces ThGraph with ThHypergraph (because SQL tables have multi-column keys and compound foreign keys) and drops ThMulti (because SQL doesn’t have native array columns in the same sense):
\[\text{ThSQLSchema} = \text{colim}(\text{ThHypergraph}, \text{ThConstraint})\]
GraphQL adds ThInterface on top of ATProto’s combination:
\[\text{ThGraphQLSchema} = \text{colim}(\text{ThGraph}, \text{ThConstraint}, \text{ThMulti}, \text{ThInterface})\]
Group E protocols—XML/XSD, HTML, Neo4j, and their kin—add ThMeta to the constrained multigraph base. This captures the fact that these formats carry an additional annotation layer (XML attributes, HTML metadata, Neo4j property maps) that sits alongside the tree structure rather than inside it:
\[\text{ThXMLSchema} = \text{colim}(\text{ThGraph}, \text{ThConstraint}, \text{ThMulti}, \text{ThMeta})\]
The instance theory for Group E protocols is correspondingly \(\text{colim}(\text{ThWType}, \text{ThMeta})\): a wellfounded tree together with a metadata functor that annotates nodes with out-of-band data. Migration for these protocols runs the standard W-type pipeline on the tree component and a separate restriction pass on the metadata component, with the two passes coupled at the nodes they share.
The pattern is modular and extensible. If tomorrow a new protocol emerges that needs graph structure, constraints, and some novel “versioning” concern, you write ThVersioning as a new theory and take the colimit with the existing pieces. The engine will derive migration, existence checks, and breaking-change detection for the new combination without any protocol-specific code.

title: 4  Protocols as Parameters
text:
4.6 The Restrict Column
The rightmost column of Table 4.1, labeled “Restrict,” names the operation that applies a migration to data. This column is determined by the instance theory—you never choose it independently.

4.6.1 Precomposition (\(\Delta_F\))
When the instance theory is ThFunctor, the restriction operator is precomposition. Given:

A schema morphism \(F : \mathcal{S}_1 \to \mathcal{S}_2\)
An instance \(I : \mathcal{S}_2 \to \mathbf{Set}\)

the restricted instance is \(\Delta_F(I) = I \circ F\). This is the pullback functor from (Spivak 2012).
In practice, precomposition is almost trivially simple to implement. For each vertex \(v\) in \(\mathcal{S}_1\), look up \(F(v)\) in \(\mathcal{S}_2\) and take the data from that vertex. For each edge \(e\) in \(\mathcal{S}_1\), look up \(F(e)\) and use that function to chase foreign keys. The functoriality of \(F\) guarantees that everything is consistent.


4.6.2 Signature Restriction + BFS + Contraction
When the instance theory is ThWType, the restriction operator is a multi-step pipeline. We gave a brief overview in Section 4.2.2; Chapter 7 provides the full algorithmic detail. The key steps are:

Signature restriction: discard tree nodes whose anchor vertex is not in the image of the migration’s vertex map.
Reachability BFS: from the root, walk the tree to find which surviving nodes are still connected.
Ancestor contraction: if a removed node had children that survived, reattach those children to the nearest surviving ancestor.
Edge resolution: relabel parent-child edges according to the migration’s edge map. If the schema allows parallel edges (multigraphs), this step may require resolver data to disambiguate.
Fan reconstruction: rebuild the node’s children list to reflect the new edge labels and ordering.

This pipeline is more expensive than precomposition, but it handles the structural complexity of tree-shaped data. Every step is necessary: skip reachability and you’ll keep orphaned subtrees; skip contraction and you’ll lose data that should survive; skip resolution and you’ll have ambiguous edge labels.


4.6.3 Field Projection
When the instance theory is ThFlat, the restriction operator reduces to field projection: keep the fields that the migration maps, drop the rest. As noted in Section 4.2.3, this is the depth-1 degenerate case of the W-type pipeline—the BFS is trivial (root plus leaves only), contraction never fires, and fan reconstruction is a simple list filter. Group C protocols (Protobuf, Avro, Thrift, Cap’n Proto, FlatBuffers, ASN.1, Bond, Redis, HCL) all use this operator.

title: 4  Protocols as Parameters
text:
4.7 From Table to Engine
The protocol table is not just a reference chart. It is a specification that the engine reads at startup. When you instantiate panproto for a particular protocol, the engine:

Parses the schema theory to determine what sorts (Vertex, Edge, Constraint, …) exist and what operations connect them.
Parses the instance theory to determine the data representation (functor, W-type, or flat).
Derives the migration type from the schema theory’s sorts and operations. A migration must carry a map for each sort in the schema theory.
Derives the restriction operator from the instance theory. Functor → precomposition. W-type → tree surgery. Flat → field projection.
Derives existence checks from the schema theory’s axioms (Chapter 6).
Derives the breaking-change classifier from the interaction of schema theory and instance theory (Chapter 9).

Steps 3–6 are fully automatic. The protocol author provides the two theories; the engine provides the rest.
This is the sense in which panproto is protocol-parametric. The protocol is a parameter—literally, a pair of theory values passed to the engine. Swap the parameter, and you get a different protocol’s migration system, with correct semantics, for free.

title: 4  Protocols as Parameters
text:
4.8 Protocols as a Design Space
The two-parameter architecture gives us a way to think about the design space of all 76 protocols. Each point in the space is a (schema theory, instance theory) pair; the six theory groups correspond to six clusters of points.






graph LR
    subgraph "Functor Instances"
        B["Group B<br/>Hypergraph + Functor<br/>(11 protocols)<br/>SQL, CQL, Parquet, Arrow, ..."]
    end
    subgraph "W-Type Instances"
        A["Group A<br/>Constrained Multigraph + W-Type<br/>(30 protocols)<br/>ATProto, JSON Schema, OpenAPI, ..."]
        D["Group D<br/>Typed Graph + W-Type + Interfaces<br/>(16 protocols)<br/>GraphQL, TypeScript, Rust Serde, ..."]
        E["Group E<br/>Constrained Multigraph + W-Type + Meta<br/>(9 protocols)<br/>XML/XSD, HTML, Neo4j, ..."]
    end
    subgraph "Flat Instances"
        C["Group C<br/>Simple Graph + Flat<br/>(9 protocols)<br/>Protobuf, Avro, Thrift, ..."]
    end




Figure 4.1: The protocol design space, showing all 76 protocols organized into six theory groups. Horizontal axis: schema theory complexity. Vertical axis: instance theory family.





This framing surfaces some natural questions:

What separates Group A from Group D? Both use ThWType instances over constrained multigraph schemas. The distinguishing feature is ThInterface: Group D protocols treat abstract types (interfaces, traits, type classes) as first-class schema citizens, whereas Group A protocols do not.
What makes Group E distinct from Group A? Group E adds ThMeta to the schema colimit and correspondingly lifts the instance theory to \(\text{colim}(\text{ThWType}, \text{ThMeta})\). The metadata layer is not just an annotation convention; it participates in migration.
What lives between Group B and Group A? A protocol with hypergraph schemas but W-type instances—perhaps a document database with compound keys and nested values. panproto can accommodate such a protocol by combining ThHypergraph, ThConstraint, and ThWType in the obvious way.
What lies beyond Group D? Add a ThVersioning theory to the schema colimit, and you get a protocol with built-in schema versioning at the theory level. The engine will derive migration and breaking-change detection for it automatically.
Can you mix instance theories? In principle, a protocol could use functor instances for some schema sorts and W-type instances for others. panproto doesn’t support this yet, but the theory permits it.

The design space is open. panproto gives you the coordinates.

title: 4  Protocols as Parameters
text:
4.9 Historical Context
The idea that data migration can be parameterized by algebraic structure has roots in several traditions.
Spivak (2012) showed that functorial data migration—using functors between categories as schema morphisms—gives a clean, compositional account of SQL-style migration. The CQL project (Schultz et al. 2017) turned this into a practical tool for relational databases, demonstrating that the category-theoretic framework could handle real workloads.
The W-type perspective comes from dependent type theory. Gambino and Hyland (2004) showed that wellfounded trees (W-types) are the natural inductive types in categories with dependent products. Abbott et al. (2005) connected this to the theory of containers, which views tree-shaped data as polynomial functors. panproto uses this connection: a W-type instance is a section of a polynomial functor determined by the schema.
The insight that these traditions can be unified under a common framework—that functor instances, W-type instances, and flat instances all arise as models of different instance theories, with migration derived from the theory structure, and that 76 real-world protocols fall cleanly into six theory groups under this classification—is, as far as we know, original to panproto.

title: 4  Protocols as Parameters
text:
4.10 Looking Ahead
We now have the vocabulary to describe any protocol as a point in a two-dimensional space, and a catalogue of 76 protocols organized into six theory groups that illustrate how that space is populated. In the next chapter, we’ll use this vocabulary to do something concrete: migrate data by hand, step by step, watching the restriction operator at work on a real schema change. This will ground the abstractions of this chapter in the specifics of ATProto records and SQL rows.

title: 4  Protocols as Parameters
text:
4.11 Summary

A protocol in panproto is a pair: (schema theory, instance theory). panproto currently supports 76 protocols organized into six theory groups.
Schema theories are assembled as colimits of simpler theories capturing orthogonal concerns (graph structure, constraints, multiplicity, interfaces, metadata, hyperedges).
Instance theories fall into three families: set-valued functors (Group B, 11 protocols), W-types (Groups A, D, E; 55 protocols total), and flat instances (Group C, 9 protocols). ThFlat is the depth-1 degenerate case of ThWType.
The restriction operator—how migration acts on data—is determined by the instance theory. Functors use precomposition (\(\Delta_F\)). W-types use a multi-step tree surgery pipeline. Flat instances use field projection, which is the depth-1 specialization of tree surgery.
Group E protocols extend the W-type machinery with a metadata theory (\(\text{colim}(\text{ThWType}, \text{ThMeta})\)), enabling migration of both tree structure and out-of-band annotation in a single pass.
Everything else is derived. Migration types, existence checks, and breaking-change classifiers are mechanically produced from the two theories.
The protocol is a parameter, not hardcoded logic. Adding a new protocol means declaring two theories, not writing an engine.







Abbott, Michael, Thorsten Altenkirch, and Neil Ghani. 2005. “Containers: Constructing Strictly Positive Types.” Theoretical Computer Science 342 (1): 3–27. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tcs.2005.06.002.


Gambino, Nicola, and J. Martin E. Hyland. 2004. “Wellfounded Trees and Dependent Polynomial Functors.” Types for Proofs and Programs (TYPES 2003), Lecture notes in computer science, vol. 3085: 210–25. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-540-24849-1_14.


Schultz, Patrick, David I. Spivak, Christina Vasilakopoulou, and Ryan Wisnesky. 2017. “Algebraic Databases.” Theory and Applications of Categories 32 (16): 547–619. http://www.tac.mta.ca/tac/volumes/32/16/32-16abs.html.


Spivak, David I. 2012. “Functorial Data Migration.” Information and Computation 217: 31–51. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ic.2012.05.001.

title: 5  Migration by Hand
text:
5.1 The Setup
The previous chapter described protocols as pairs of theories and promised that migration falls out automatically. That’s true, but abstractions are only useful if you can trace them through a concrete example. In this chapter, we will migrate a real schema change—adding a tags field to a Bluesky post—step by step, by hand, watching every piece of the machinery at work.
By the end, you will be able to look at any schema change and predict exactly what the migration engine will do. That predictability is the whole point: migration should never be surprising.
We start with a minimal ATProto schema for a post. Version 1 has two fields: text (a string containing the post body) and createdAt (a datetime timestamp).
This schema, viewed through the lens of Chapter 4, is a model of the ATProto schema theory: colimit(ThGraph, ThConstraint, ThMulti). Concretely, it’s a directed graph with:
Now suppose we want version 2, which adds a tags field—an array of strings that let users categorize their posts.
The v2 schema has an additional vertex (Tags, with kind array<string>) and an additional edge (tags : Post → Tags, with multiplicity optional). Everything else is unchanged.
The question is: given a record conforming to v2, how do we produce a record conforming to v1?

title: 5  Migration by Hand
5  Migration by Hand
text:
5.2 The Migration Morphism
A migration from v1 to v2 is a schema morphism: a structure-preserving map from the v1 schema graph to the v2 schema graph. In panproto, this means specifying a vertex map and an edge map.
const migration = panproto.migration(schemaV1, schemaV2)
  .map('post', 'post')
  .map('post:body', 'post:body')
  .map('post:body.text', 'post:body.text')
  .map('post:body.createdAt', 'post:body.createdAt')
  .compile();
Let’s read this carefully:
Vertex map: each v1 vertex is mapped to the v2 vertex it corresponds to.

Post ↦ Post — the post record type is the same in both versions.
Text ↦ Text — the text type is the same.
CreatedAt ↦ CreatedAt — the datetime type is the same.

Edge map: each v1 edge is mapped to the v2 edge it corresponds to.

text ↦ text — the text field is the same.
createdAt ↦ createdAt — the createdAt field is the same.

Notice what is not in the migration: the Tags vertex and the tags edge. They exist in v2 but have no preimage in v1. This is the structural encoding of “v2 added a new field that v1 doesn’t know about.”
The migration morphism is an injection from the v1 schema into the v2 schema. Not all migrations are injections—you can merge vertices, collapse edges, remap types—but the “add a field” pattern always produces an injection.

title: 5  Migration by Hand
text:
5.3 What the Maps Mean
It’s worth dwelling on the semantics of these maps, because they carry more information than they might appear to at first glance.
The vertex map \(\phi_V : V_1 \to V_2\) says: “for each type in the old schema, here is the corresponding type in the new schema.” If a v1 vertex maps to a v2 vertex, the engine knows that data anchored to that vertex type can survive the migration. If a v2 vertex has no preimage under \(\phi_V\), the engine knows that data anchored to that vertex type must be introduced (in the forward direction) or discarded (in the backward direction).
The edge map \(\phi_E : E_1 \to E_2\) says: “for each field/relationship in the old schema, here is the corresponding field/relationship in the new schema.” This map must be compatible with the vertex map: if edge \(e_1\) goes from \(u_1\) to \(v_1\), then \(\phi_E(e_1)\) must go from \(\phi_V(u_1)\) to \(\phi_V(v_1)\). This compatibility condition ensures that the migration is structure-preserving—it doesn’t scramble the graph topology.
Together, \((\phi_V, \phi_E)\) form a graph homomorphism from the v1 schema to the v2 schema. In the language of (Spivak 2012), this is a functor between the schemas viewed as categories (though for ATProto, which uses a graph rather than a category as its schema theory, the precise statement is a graph homomorphism).

title: 5  Migration by Hand
5  Migration by Hand
text:
5.5 Tracing the Lift
Suppose we have a v2 record:
{
  "text": "Hello, world!",
  "createdAt": "2025-01-15T12:00:00Z",
  "tags": ["greeting", "test"]
}
This record, viewed as a W-type instance, is a tree:






graph TD
    R["Post (root)"]
    T["Text: 'Hello, world!'"]
    C["CreatedAt: '2025-01-15T12:00:00Z'"]
    G["Tags: ['greeting', 'test']"]
    R -->|text| T
    R -->|createdAt| C
    R -->|tags| G




Figure 5.1: The v2 record as a W-type tree.





The root node is anchored to the Post vertex. It has three children, each anchored to a different vertex and reached via a different edge.
Now the engine applies the lift. Because ATProto uses W-type instances, it runs the tree surgery pipeline:

5.5.1 Step 1: Signature Restriction
Remove nodes anchored to vertices not in the surviving vertex set. The surviving set is {Post, Text, CreatedAt}. The Tags vertex is not in this set, so the node anchored to Tags is removed:






graph TD
    R["Post (root)"]
    T["Text: 'Hello, world!'"]
    C["CreatedAt: '2025-01-15T12:00:00Z'"]
    R -->|text| T
    R -->|createdAt| C




Figure 5.2: After signature restriction: the Tags node is removed.







5.5.2 Step 2: Reachability BFS
Starting from the root, walk the tree to find all surviving nodes that are still reachable. In this simple case, both remaining children are direct children of the root, so they’re trivially reachable. No nodes are lost.
In more complex migrations—where intermediate nodes are removed—this step can discover orphaned subtrees that need to be either discarded or reattached via ancestor contraction.


5.5.3 Step 3: Ancestor Contraction
When a removed node has surviving descendants, those descendants need to be reattached to the nearest surviving ancestor. In our example, the removed Tags node has no surviving descendants (it was a leaf), so contraction is a no-op.
We will see contraction do real work in Chapter 7, where we handle nested schema changes.


5.5.4 Step 4: Edge Resolution
Relabel edges according to the migration’s edge map. Since our edge map is the identity on surviving edges (text ↦ text, createdAt ↦ createdAt), the labels don’t change.
Edge resolution becomes interesting when the migration renames fields, or when ancestor contraction introduces new parent-child relationships that need to be labeled.


5.5.5 Step 5: Fan Reconstruction
Rebuild the children list for each surviving node. The root’s fan (its set of outgoing edges and their targets) is now {text → Text, createdAt → CreatedAt}, which is exactly the fan described by the v1 schema.
The result is a v1-compatible record:
{
  "text": "Hello, world!",
  "createdAt": "2025-01-15T12:00:00Z"
}
The tags field has been cleanly removed. The surviving fields are untouched.

title: 5  Migration by Hand
text:
5.6 What Happened Under the Hood
Let’s step back and name what just occurred. The migration morphism \((\phi_V, \phi_E) : S_1 \hookrightarrow S_2\) is an injection from the v1 schema into the v2 schema. The lift operation took a v2 instance (a tree) and produced a v1 instance (a smaller tree) by:

Restricting to the image of \(\phi_V\) (keeping only the types that v1 knows about).
Checking reachability (making sure nothing got disconnected).
Contracting past removed intermediaries (not needed here).
Resolving edge labels (trivial here).
Rebuilding the fan structure (trivial here).

This five-step pipeline is the restriction operator for W-type instances that we mentioned in Chapter 4. For this particular migration—adding a leaf field—the pipeline reduces to “delete the new node.” But the same pipeline handles arbitrarily complex schema changes: renaming fields, restructuring nested objects, collapsing hierarchies, splitting types. The steps are always the same; only the work done in each step varies.

title: 5  Migration by Hand
text:
5.7 The Same Pattern in SQL
To build intuition across protocol boundaries, let’s see the same “add a column” migration in SQL, where the instance theory is ThFunctor instead of ThWType.
v1 schema (as a category):

Objects: posts (a table)
No non-identity morphisms (no foreign keys in this simple example)

v2 schema:

Objects: posts (same table, now with an additional tags column)

Migration: the functor \(F : \mathcal{S}_1 \to \mathcal{S}_2\) maps posts ↦ posts.
Instance: a v2 instance \(I : \mathcal{S}_2 \to \mathbf{Set}\) assigns to posts the set of all rows:



id
text
created_at
tags




1
“Hello, world!”
2025-01-15
{“greeting”, “test”}


2
“Another post”
2025-01-16
{}



Lift (\(\Delta_F\)): the restricted instance \(\Delta_F(I) = I \circ F\) assigns to posts the same set of rows, but viewed through the v1 schema, which doesn’t include the tags column. The result is the projection:



id
text
created_at




1
“Hello, world!”
2025-01-15


2
“Another post”
2025-01-16



The tags column is dropped because v1’s schema doesn’t have a morphism corresponding to it.






NoteSame migration, different mechanics



The ATProto and SQL versions of “add a field” produce the same observable result: the new field is dropped when migrating backward. But the mechanism is different. ATProto prunes a tree node (W-type restriction). SQL projects away a column (functor precomposition). The migration morphism is the same kind of object in both cases—a structure-preserving map between schemas—but the restriction operator is determined by the instance theory.
This is the two-parameter architecture at work: the schema morphism is protocol-independent, but the data operation is protocol-specific, derived from the instance theory.

title: 5  Migration by Hand
text:
5.8 Composing Migrations
Migrations compose. If you have a migration \(m_{12} : S_1 \to S_2\) and a migration \(m_{23} : S_2 \to S_3\), their composite \(m_{13} = m_{23} \circ m_{12} : S_1 \to S_3\) is also a valid migration.
For our running example, suppose v3 adds a langs field (an array of language codes) on top of v2:

\(m_{12}\) : v1 → v2 (adds tags)
\(m_{23}\) : v2 → v3 (adds langs)
\(m_{13} = m_{23} \circ m_{12}\) : v1 → v3 (adds both tags and langs)

The composite migration’s vertex map sends each v1 vertex through both maps: \(\phi^{13}_V(v) = \phi^{23}_V(\phi^{12}_V(v))\). The edge map composes similarly.
Lifting through the composite is equivalent to lifting through each step in sequence:
\[\text{lift}(m_{13}, \text{record}_3) = \text{lift}(m_{12}, \text{lift}(m_{23}, \text{record}_3))\]
For functor instances, this is automatic: \(\Delta_{F \circ G} = \Delta_G \circ \Delta_F\) (precomposition is contravariantly functorial). For W-type instances, the engine must verify this property for each migration pair, but in practice it holds whenever both migrations pass the existence checks of Chapter 6.
Composability is what makes migration scalable. You don’t need a migration from every old version to every new version. You maintain a chain \(S_1 \to S_2 \to \cdots \to S_n\), and the engine composes as needed.

title: 5  Migration by Hand
section: 5.9 Migrations That Do More Than Add Fields
text:
5.9 Migrations That Do More Than Add Fields
Adding a field is the simplest possible migration. Let’s briefly sketch what happens for more complex schema changes, to give you a sense of the pipeline’s range.

5.9.1 Renaming a Field
Suppose v2 renames text to body. The vertex map still sends Text ↦ Text (the type hasn’t changed), but the edge map sends text ↦ body. During edge resolution (step 4), the engine relabels the body edge back to text, producing a record that uses the old field name. The data is untouched; only the label changes.


5.9.2 Restructuring: Extracting a Nested Object
Suppose v1 has a flat post with text, createdAt, and authorName. v2 extracts authorName into a nested author object with a name field. The vertex map sends v1’s AuthorName to v2’s Name (inside the Author object). The edge map sends v1’s authorName to the composed path author.name.
During ancestor contraction (step 3), the engine recognizes that the Author vertex is an intermediate node not in v1’s schema. It contracts the path Post → Author → Name to Post → AuthorName, preserving the data while flattening the structure.


5.9.3 Removing a Required Field
Suppose v2 removes the createdAt field entirely. The vertex map doesn’t include CreatedAt, and the edge map doesn’t include createdAt. This migration might not exist—if v1 requires createdAt (it’s not optional), then there’s no valid way to produce a v1 record without it. The engine detects this during compilation and reports an existence failure. Chapter 6 covers the precise conditions.

title: 5  Migration by Hand
section: 5.10 The Lift as a Natural Transformation
text:
5.10 The Lift as a Natural Transformation
For readers with a category theory background, there is a clean way to state what lift does. The lift operation is the component of a natural transformation between data presheaves, induced by the migration morphism.
In the functor case, this is literal. A migration \(F : \mathcal{S}_1 \to \mathcal{S}_2\) induces a functor \(\Delta_F : \mathbf{Set}^{\mathcal{S}_2} \to \mathbf{Set}^{\mathcal{S}_1}\) between presheaf categories. This functor sends each \(\mathcal{S}_2\)-instance to an \(\mathcal{S}_1\)-instance by precomposition. The lift of a single record is the action of \(\Delta_F\) on a representable presheaf (Spivak 2012).
In the W-type case, the connection is less direct but still meaningful. The tree surgery pipeline computes the action of a restriction functor on the polynomial functor associated with the schema. The mathematical details involve the theory of polynomial functors and their morphisms (Gambino and Hyland 2004; Abbott et al. 2005), which we defer to Appendix C.
For practical purposes, you don’t need the category theory to use panproto effectively. The compile + lift API is self-contained. But if you want to verify that a complex migration is correct, the categorical perspective gives you the tools: check that the morphism is a valid graph homomorphism, and the rest follows by functoriality.

title: 5  Migration by Hand
text:
5.11 A Note on Terminology
Different communities use different words for the operations we’ve described:



Table 5.1: Terminology across communities.











panproto

Cambria

panproto’s terminology is closest to Litt et al. (2022)’s Cambria project, which was an important practical inspiration. The key difference is that Cambria hardcodes its schema language and migration semantics, while panproto parameterizes both by theories.

title: 5  Migration by Hand
text:
5.12 Cross-Format Migration: Parse, Migrate, Emit
Everything in this chapter has focused on migration within a single protocol: a v1 ATProto schema to a v2 ATProto schema, or a v1 SQL schema to a v2 SQL schema. But the same machinery supports migration across protocols.
Because panproto represents every schema as a graph regardless of its origin format, you can migrate between formats by composing three steps:

Parse a Protobuf schema into a schema graph (a model of the Protobuf theory, colimit(ThSimpleGraph, ThConstraint)).
Build a migration morphism that maps Protobuf concepts to Avro concepts—vertex by vertex and edge by edge—producing a graph homomorphism from the Protobuf schema theory into the Avro schema theory.
Emit the result as an Avro schema by applying the Avro emitter to the target graph.

The migration morphism in step 2 is the same kind of object as the intra-protocol migrations we’ve been building throughout this chapter. What differs is that the source and target schemas now belong to different protocols. panproto validates that the morphism respects both theories and that any constraints present in the source are satisfiable in the target.
See Chapter 13 for a comprehensive treatment of cross-format conversion.

title: 5  Migration by Hand
text:
5.13 Summary

A migration is a structure-preserving map (vertex map + edge map) from an old schema to a new schema.
The migration is compiled to pre-compute surviving sets and remap tables.
The lift operation applies the compiled migration to a record, producing a record conforming to the old schema.
For W-type instances (ATProto, JSON, GraphQL), lift runs a 5-step tree surgery pipeline: signature restriction, reachability BFS, ancestor contraction, edge resolution, fan reconstruction.
For functor instances (SQL, CQL), lift is precomposition: pull rows through the vertex map.
Migrations compose: you maintain a chain of migrations, and the engine composes as needed.
The same migration morphism structure works across protocols; only the lift implementation varies, determined by the instance theory.







Abbott, Michael, Thorsten Altenkirch, and Neil Ghani. 2005. “Containers: Constructing Strictly Positive Types.” Theoretical Computer Science 342 (1): 3–27. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tcs.2005.06.002.


Gambino, Nicola, and J. Martin E. Hyland. 2004. “Wellfounded Trees and Dependent Polynomial Functors.” Types for Proofs and Programs (TYPES 2003), Lecture notes in computer science, vol. 3085: 210–25. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-540-24849-1_14.


Litt, Geoffrey, Martin Kleppmann, and Marc Shapiro. 2022. Project Cambria: Schema Evolution for CRDTs. Ink & Switch research essay. https://www.inkandswitch.com/cambria/.


Spivak, David I. 2012. “Functorial Data Migration.” Information and Computation 217: 31–51. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ic.2012.05.001.

title: 6  When Migrations Exist
text:
6.1 Theory-Derived Conditions
Not every proposed vertex map and edge map constitutes a valid migration. Some schema changes are structurally incoherent—they ask the engine to do something impossible, like satisfy a tighter constraint with no new data, or resolve an ambiguous edge label with no disambiguating information. The engine must detect these situations before attempting to lift data, because a migration that silently produces garbage is worse than one that refuses to run.
In most migration tools, the validity checks are hardcoded: a fixed list of rules, written by hand, specific to one protocol. panproto takes a different approach. The existence conditions are derived from the schema theory and instance theory. The engine inspects the theory’s sorts, operations, and axioms, and assembles the appropriate checks automatically. This is why the same engine can validate migrations for SQL (where you need foreign key consistency) and ATProto (where you need reachability in a tree).
This chapter explains how that derivation works and demonstrates it through four worked examples of migrations that fail.
The engine determines which existence checks to apply by inspecting the schema theory’s signature—its sorts and operations. Each sort or structural feature triggers a specific check. The checks are cumulative: a schema theory with more sorts requires more checks.
Here is the mapping from theory features to existence conditions:

title: 6  When Migrations Exist
text:
6.1.1 Kind Consistency
Trigger: the schema theory has a Kind sort (a sort classifying vertices by their data type).
Condition: for every vertex \(v\) in the source schema, \(\text{kind}(v)\) must be compatible with \(\text{kind}(\phi_V(v))\) in the target schema. “Compatible” means the kinds are equal, or the source kind is a subtype of the target kind according to the protocol’s subtyping rules.
Intuition: you can’t map a string vertex to a union{string, integer} vertex if the protocol doesn’t support implicit widening. The kinds must match (or the target must be strictly more general, in protocols that support subsumption).
Protocols affected: all protocols have some notion of kind. The strictness of “compatible” varies: Protobuf is strict (kinds must be equal), ATProto allows some subtyping (string is compatible with union{string}), GraphQL respects interface subtyping.


6.1.2 Edge Compatibility
Trigger: the schema theory has an Edge sort with src and tgt operations (operations assigning source and target vertices to each edge).
Condition: the edge map must be compatible with the vertex map. Formally, for every edge \(e : u \to v\) in the source schema:
\[\text{src}(\phi_E(e)) = \phi_V(\text{src}(e)) \quad \text{and} \quad \text{tgt}(\phi_E(e)) = \phi_V(\text{tgt}(e))\]
This is the naturality condition: the edge map and vertex map must form a graph homomorphism.
Intuition: if field text goes from Post to Text in v1, and you map Post ↦ Post and Text ↦ Text, then you must map text to an edge that also goes from Post to Text. You can’t map it to an edge from Post to Integer—that would break the graph structure.
Protocols affected: all protocols with edges (which is all of them—edges encode fields and relationships).


6.1.3 Constraint Compatibility
Trigger: the schema theory has a Constraint sort (a sort attaching invariants to vertices or edges).
Condition: for every constraint \(c\) on a source vertex or edge, the corresponding constraint in the target must be at least as permissive. That is, the set of values satisfying the target constraint must be a superset of the values satisfying the source constraint.
More precisely, if \(c_1\) constrains vertex \(v\) in the source schema, and \(c_2\) constrains \(\phi_V(v)\) in the target schema, then the engine checks:
\[\text{sat}(c_2) \supseteq \text{sat}(c_1)\]
where \(\text{sat}(c)\) is the set of values satisfying constraint \(c\).
Intuition: if the source schema says maxLength: 3000 and the target says maxLength: 300, then a valid source value of length 1000 would violate the target constraint. The migration can’t guarantee that all source data fits in the target’s tighter constraint, so it fails.
The direction here matters. We are checking whether target data can be restricted to the source schema. A tighter target constraint is fine (all target values already satisfy the source constraint). A looser target constraint is the problem: the source expects tight bounds, but the target allows values that would violate them.
Wait—let’s be precise. In the backward direction (lifting v2 data to v1), we need v2 data to satisfy v1’s constraints. So the check is: for each source (v1) constraint, every value that satisfies the target (v2) constraint must also satisfy the source constraint. This means \(\text{sat}(c_2) \subseteq \text{sat}(c_1)\)—the target must be tighter or equal to the source.
For the forward direction, the condition reverses. panproto checks the appropriate direction based on which lift direction you’re compiling.
Protocols affected: ATProto, SQL, Protobuf, GraphQL, JSON Schema—every protocol with constraints.


6.1.4 Pushout Complement Uniqueness
Trigger: the schema theory allows parallel edges (the multigraph condition from ThMulti).
Condition: when ancestor contraction creates a new parent-child relationship (because an intermediate node was removed), there must be a unique way to label the new edge. If the schema allows multiple edges between the same pair of vertices, the engine cannot determine which label to use without additional resolver data.
This condition comes from the theory of algebraic graph transformation. In the double-pushout (DPO) approach (Ehrig et al. 2006), a graph rewriting rule can only be applied when a pushout complement exists, and that complement is unique. The pushout complement existence condition—also called the gluing condition—ensures that the rewriting doesn’t create dangling edges or ambiguous identifications.
In panproto’s setting, the analogous condition arises during ancestor contraction. When a removed intermediate vertex \(w\) has a parent edge \(e_1 : u \to w\) and a child edge \(e_2 : w \to v\), contraction needs to create a direct edge \(e' : u \to v\). If the target schema has multiple edges from \(\phi_V(u)\) to \(\phi_V(v)\), the choice of \(e'\) is ambiguous.
Intuition: imagine a schema where a Person has two edges to Address: homeAddress and workAddress. If a migration removes an intermediate ContactInfo node, the engine needs to reconnect Person to Address directly, but which label should it use? Without resolver data, the migration is ambiguous and therefore invalid.
Protocols affected: ATProto, GraphQL, JSON Schema (all protocols with ThMulti in the schema theory).


6.1.5 Signature Coherence and Simultaneity
Trigger: the schema theory has a HyperEdge sort (edges connecting more than two vertices simultaneously).
Condition: when a migration maps the vertices of a hyperedge, all vertices in the hyperedge must be mapped, or none of them. You cannot partially map a hyperedge—that would create a dangling hyperedge connecting some mapped vertices and some unmapped ones.
This is the simultaneity condition for hypergraph morphisms, discussed by Barr and Wells (1990) in the context of sketch morphisms. A sketch morphism must preserve the distinguished cones and cocones of the sketch, which requires that the underlying graph homomorphism respect the hypergraph structure.
Intuition: in SQL, a compound foreign key (author_id, post_id) → (users.id, posts.id) is a hyperedge connecting three tables simultaneously. If you map users and posts but drop the join table, the foreign key becomes incoherent. Either all participants in the hyperedge survive, or the hyperedge itself must be explicitly removed.
Protocols affected: SQL (the primary user of ThHyperEdge).


6.1.6 Reachability
Trigger: the instance theory uses W-types (ThWType).
Condition: after applying the vertex map, the root vertex of every instance must still be reachable. More precisely, for every path from the root to a leaf in the source schema, the image of that path under the migration must still be a valid path in the target schema (possibly shortened by contraction, but never broken).
This is a global condition: it cannot be checked vertex-by-vertex. The engine performs a reachability analysis on the target schema, restricted to the image of the migration, starting from the image of the root vertex.
Intuition: if a migration removes a vertex that lies on every path from the root to some subtree, that entire subtree becomes unreachable. The data it contains cannot be preserved. If the subtree contains required fields, the migration is invalid.
Protocols affected: ATProto, GraphQL, JSON Schema (all protocols with W-type instances).

title: 6  When Migrations Exist
text:
6.2 The Check Pipeline
The engine assembles these checks into a pipeline based on the theory structure. The pipeline runs during compile and reports all failures at once (it does not short-circuit on the first failure, because developers need to see the full picture).






flowchart TD
    M["Migration morphism (φ_V, φ_E)"] --> K{Kind sort?}
    K -->|yes| KC["Kind consistency check"]
    K -->|no| E
    KC --> E{Edge sort?}
    E -->|yes| EC["Edge compatibility check"]
    E -->|no| C
    EC --> C{Constraint sort?}
    C -->|yes| CC["Constraint compatibility check"]
    C -->|no| P
    CC --> P{Parallel edges?}
    P -->|yes| PC["Pushout complement uniqueness"]
    P -->|no| H
    PC --> H{HyperEdge sort?}
    H -->|yes| HC["Signature coherence + simultaneity"]
    H -->|no| R
    HC --> R{W-type instances?}
    R -->|yes| RC["Reachability check"]
    R -->|no| OK["✓ Migration exists"]
    RC --> OK
    KC -.->|fail| F["✗ Migration does not exist"]
    EC -.->|fail| F
    CC -.->|fail| F
    PC -.->|fail| F
    HC -.->|fail| F
    RC -.->|fail| F




Figure 6.1: The existence check pipeline. Each check is triggered by a theory feature.





The pipeline is data-driven: the engine reads the theory and builds the pipeline at initialization time. Adding a new sort to a theory automatically adds the corresponding check. Removing a sort removes the check. There is no hardcoded list of protocol-specific rules.

title: 6  When Migrations Exist
6  When Migrations Exist
text:
6.4 Worked Example 2: Kind Inconsistency
Consider a migration that maps a string vertex to a union{string, integer} vertex.
In ATProto’s schema theory, vertex kinds form a lattice. The kind string is not a subtype of union{string, integer}—rather, string is a component of the union, but the kinds themselves are different. An ATProto record anchored to a union{string, integer} vertex can hold either a string or an integer; a record anchored to a string vertex can only hold a string.
The migration maps StringField ↦ UnionField in the vertex map. The engine checks kind consistency:
kind(StringField) = string
kind(φ_V(StringField)) = kind(UnionField) = union{string, integer}
string ≠ union{string, integer}
The engine then consults the protocol’s subtyping rules. ATProto permits widening from a concrete type to a union containing that type in the forward direction (a string value is a valid union value). But in the backward direction, a union value might be an integer, which is not a valid string.
So this migration:

Passes kind consistency for the forward direction (string embeds into union{string, integer}).
Fails kind consistency for the backward direction (union{string, integer} does not embed into string).

The engine reports:
ExistenceError: Kind inconsistency on vertex StringField
  Source kind: string
  Target kind: union{string, integer}
  Direction: backward
  Reason: target kind is not a subkind of source kind
The fix depends on your intent. If you want backward compatibility, you need a resolver—a function that handles the integer case (e.g., by converting it to a string representation, or by signaling an error). Resolvers are covered in Chapter 8.

title: 6  When Migrations Exist
text:
6.5 Worked Example 3: Required Field Missing
This is perhaps the most common migration failure in practice. Consider v1 with a required name field and v2 without it. A migration maps v2’s vertices into v1, but name has no preimage—no v2 vertex maps to v1’s Name vertex.
In the backward direction (lifting v2 data to v1), the engine needs to produce a v1 record with a name field. But v2 data doesn’t contain a name. If name is optional in v1, this is fine—the engine produces a record with name absent. But if name is required, the engine cannot produce a valid v1 record.
The check works as follows:

The engine identifies all v1 vertices and edges that are not in the image of the migration.
For each unmapped vertex, it checks whether the vertex is required (reachable from the root via a path of required edges).
If an unmapped vertex is required, the migration fails.

This check is a combination of edge compatibility and reachability. The engine uses the schema theory’s constraint sort (to determine which edges are required vs. optional) and, for W-type instances, performs a reachability analysis from the root.
ExistenceError: Required field missing
  Unmapped vertex: Name (kind: string)
  Required path: Post → name → Name
  Direction: backward
  Reason: no source vertex maps to Name, and Name is reachable
          via a required path from the root
The fix is to provide a default value for the missing field. In panproto, defaults are specified as part of the migration morphism:
// Adding a default makes the migration valid
const migration = {
  vertexMap: { /* ... existing maps ... */ },
  edgeMap: { /* ... existing maps ... */ },
  defaults: {
    Name: { value: "Unknown" }
  }
};
With the default in place, the lift operation fills in "Unknown" for the missing name field, and the migration passes.

title: 6  When Migrations Exist
text:
6.6 Worked Example 4: Pushout Complement Ambiguity
This example requires a multigraph schema—one with parallel edges between the same pair of vertices. ATProto, GraphQL, and JSON Schema all allow this (via ThMulti in the schema theory).
Consider a schema with:

Vertices: Person, ContactInfo, Address
Edges: contact : Person → ContactInfo, location : ContactInfo → Address

And a v2 schema that removes ContactInfo, connecting Person directly to Address via two edges:

Vertices: Person, Address
Edges: homeAddress : Person → Address, workAddress : Person → Address

The migration maps Person ↦ Person and Address ↦ Address, but ContactInfo has no preimage (it’s been removed). During ancestor contraction, the engine needs to create a direct edge from Person to Address to replace the two-hop path Person → ContactInfo → Address.
But there are two edges from Person to Address in v2: homeAddress and workAddress. Which one should the contracted edge map to?
The engine cannot decide. This is the pushout complement uniqueness failure—the contraction produces an ambiguous edge.
ExistenceError: Pushout complement ambiguity
  Contracted path: Person → ContactInfo → Address
  Candidate edges: homeAddress, workAddress
  Direction: backward
  Reason: ancestor contraction produces ambiguous edge label;
          multiple edges from Person to Address in target schema
This failure has its roots in algebraic graph transformation theory. Ehrig et al. (2006) showed that DPO graph rewriting requires the pushout complement to exist and be unique. When it’s not unique, the rewriting is ambiguous. Löwe (1993) studied the single-pushout (SPO) approach, which resolves ambiguity by making a default choice (typically deletion), but panproto follows the DPO tradition and requires explicit disambiguation.
The fix is to provide resolver data in the migration morphism:
const migration = {
  vertexMap: { Person: "Person", Address: "Address" },
  edgeMap: { /* ... */ },
  resolvers: {
    // When contracting Person → ContactInfo → Address,
    // use the homeAddress edge
    "Person→ContactInfo→Address": "homeAddress"
  }
};
With the resolver, the engine knows which edge to use, and the migration passes. Resolvers are discussed more fully in Chapter 8.

title: 6  When Migrations Exist
text:
6.7 Why Derivation Matters
You might wonder: is the theory-derived approach actually better than a hardcoded list of checks? After all, the checks themselves are not complicated—constraint compatibility, kind consistency, and so on are individually straightforward.
The advantage is extensibility and correctness. Consider what happens when you add a new protocol.
With hardcoded checks: you study the new protocol’s semantics, identify the relevant existence conditions, write the checks by hand, and hope you haven’t missed any. If the protocol has unusual features (like hyperedges, or a novel subtyping relation), you need to invent new checks from scratch. The check suite grows linearly with the number of protocols, and cross-protocol consistency is a manual audit.
With theory-derived checks: you write the schema theory and instance theory for the new protocol. The engine reads the theory’s sorts and operations, matches them to the catalogue of known check generators, and assembles the pipeline automatically. If the theory has a Constraint sort, the constraint check fires. If the theory has parallel edges, the pushout complement check fires. You get exactly the checks that the theory requires, no more and no less.
This is a direct consequence of the framework described by Lack and Sobociński (2005): in adhesive categories (which include the categories of graphs and hypergraphs that panproto works with), the existence of pushout complements is decidable, and the conditions are determined by the categorical structure. panproto mechanizes this decidability.






NoteThe key insight



Other tools hardcode their existence checks. panproto derives them from the theory. This is why the same engine works for SQL (where you need foreign key consistency and hyperedge simultaneity) and ATProto (where you need tree reachability and pushout complement uniqueness). The checks are not the same, but the mechanism that produces them is.

title: 6  When Migrations Exist
text:
6.8 Existence vs. Uniqueness
There is a subtlety we’ve been glossing over. The checks above determine whether a migration exists—whether there is any valid way to lift data through the proposed morphism. But in some cases, a migration can exist without being unique: there might be multiple valid ways to lift the data, and the engine needs to choose one.
Uniqueness failures are strictly less severe than existence failures. An existence failure means “this migration is impossible.” A uniqueness failure means “this migration is possible but ambiguous.” The engine handles them differently:

Existence failure: the engine rejects the migration and reports the error. No data is produced.
Uniqueness failure: the engine requests resolver data. With resolvers, the migration becomes unique and can proceed.

The pushout complement ambiguity (Worked Example 4) is actually a uniqueness failure rather than an existence failure. The migration could proceed by choosing either homeAddress or workAddress—it just can’t proceed without being told which one. The resolver provides the missing information.
In the terminology of algebraic graph transformation: existence corresponds to the gluing condition (Ehrig et al. 2006), and uniqueness corresponds to the identification condition. Both must be satisfied for a DPO rewriting step to be well-defined.

title: 6  When Migrations Exist
text:
6.9 The Role of Axioms
So far we’ve focused on the theory’s signature (sorts and operations) as the trigger for existence checks. But the theory’s axioms also play a role. Axioms constrain how sorts and operations interact, and these constraints can strengthen or weaken the existence conditions.
For example, the schema theory for CQL includes the axiom that composition of morphisms is associative and unital (the category axioms). This axiom implies that the edge compatibility check is stronger for CQL than for, say, ATProto: in CQL, you must preserve not just individual edges but entire composable paths.
Similarly, if a schema theory includes an axiom stating that every vertex has a unique kind (a functionality axiom for the kind operation), then the kind consistency check can use equality rather than subtyping.
The engine reads the axioms and parameterizes the checks accordingly. In practice, most axioms affect the strictness of a check rather than its presence: the check fires because the sort exists, and the axiom determines how strictly it fires.

title: 6  When Migrations Exist
section: 6.10 Putting It Together: A Decision Procedure
text:
6.10 Putting It Together: A Decision Procedure
Given a proposed migration morphism \((\phi_V, \phi_E)\) between two schemas \(S_1\) and \(S_2\), with schema theory \(T_S\) and instance theory \(T_I\), the engine runs the following decision procedure:

Read the sorts of \(T_S\). For each sort, look up the corresponding check generator.
Read the axioms of \(T_S\). For each axiom, determine how it parameterizes the relevant checks.
Read \(T_I\) to determine whether the reachability check applies (W-type instances) or not (functor instances).
Run all applicable checks against \((\phi_V, \phi_E)\), \(S_1\), and \(S_2\).
Collect results:

All checks pass → migration exists and is unique. Proceed.
Some checks fail as existence errors → migration does not exist. Report errors.
Some checks fail as uniqueness errors → migration exists but is ambiguous. Request resolvers.

If resolvers are provided, re-run the uniqueness checks with resolver data. If they now pass, proceed.

This procedure is total: it always terminates with a definite answer. It is sound: if it says the migration exists, the lift operation will produce valid output. And it is derived: the checks it runs are determined by the theory, not by protocol-specific code.

title: 6  When Migrations Exist
text:
6.11 Comparison with Other Tools
To appreciate the theory-derived approach, it’s helpful to see how other tools handle existence checking.
CQL (Schultz et al. 2017) has rigorous existence checking, but only for functor instances over categories. It checks functoriality of the schema morphism (which subsumes edge compatibility and kind consistency in the categorical setting) and relies on the properties of adhesive categories for pushout complement existence. panproto generalizes this to arbitrary schema theories.
Cambria (Litt et al. 2022) does not have explicit existence checking. It uses a lens combinator language and relies on the programmer to compose valid lenses. Invalid lenses produce runtime errors. panproto’s compile-time checks catch these errors before any data is processed.
SQL DDL (ALTER TABLE, etc.) performs some existence checking (you can’t drop a column that’s referenced by a foreign key without CASCADE), but the checks are ad hoc and specific to the relational model. panproto derives analogous checks from the theory.
Protobuf has compatibility rules (field numbers, wire types) that function as existence checks, but they’re hardcoded in the protobuf compiler. panproto derives equivalent checks from Protobuf’s schema theory.
The advantage of the theory-derived approach is not that it produces different checks—in most cases, the checks are equivalent to what you’d write by hand. The advantage is that the checks are guaranteed to be complete for the given theory. If you miss a check in a hardcoded system, you’ll discover it when a migration silently corrupts data. In panproto, the engine derives all necessary checks from the theory, so completeness is structural rather than aspirational.

title: 6  When Migrations Exist
text:
6.12 Summary

Existence conditions determine whether a proposed migration can be validly applied to data. Not every vertex/edge map is a valid migration.
The conditions are derived from the theory: the engine inspects the schema theory’s sorts and axioms to determine which checks apply.
Six families of checks exist:

Kind consistency (triggered by Kind sort)
Edge compatibility (triggered by Edge sort with src/tgt)
Constraint compatibility (triggered by Constraint sort)
Pushout complement uniqueness (triggered by parallel edges / ThMulti)
Signature coherence + simultaneity (triggered by HyperEdge sort)
Reachability (triggered by W-type instance theory)

Existence failures are fatal: the migration cannot proceed. Uniqueness failures can be resolved with resolver data.
The decision procedure is total, sound, and derived—it always terminates, never produces invalid output, and never requires protocol-specific code.







Barr, Michael, and Charles Wells. 1990. Category Theory for Computing Science. Prentice Hall.


Ehrig, Hartmut, Karsten Ehrig, Ulrike Prange, and Gabriele Taentzer. 2006. Fundamentals of Algebraic Graph Transformation. Monographs in Theoretical Computer Science. Springer. https://doi.org/10.1007/3-540-31188-2.


Lack, Stephen, and Paweł Sobociński. 2005. “Adhesive and Quasiadhesive Categories.” RAIRO – Theoretical Informatics and Applications 39 (3): 511–45. https://doi.org/10.1051/ita:2005028.


Litt, Geoffrey, Martin Kleppmann, and Marc Shapiro. 2022. Project Cambria: Schema Evolution for CRDTs. Ink & Switch research essay. https://www.inkandswitch.com/cambria/.


Löwe, Michael. 1993. “Algebraic Approach to Single-Pushout Graph Transformation.” Theoretical Computer Science 109 (1–2): 181–224. https://doi.org/10.1016/0304-3975(93)90068-5.


Schultz, Patrick, David I. Spivak, Christina Vasilakopoulou, and Ryan Wisnesky. 2017. “Algebraic Databases.” Theory and Applications of Categories 32 (16): 547–619. http://www.tac.mta.ca/tac/volumes/32/16/32-16abs.html.

title: 7  Lifting Data
text:
7.1 A worked example: threadViewPost
The previous chapter established when a migration exists. This chapter is about how it works: the restrict operation that transforms data from one schema to another. This is the algorithmic heart of panproto.
There are two flavors of restriction, corresponding to the two kinds of schema that panproto supports. W-type restrict handles tree-structured data (JSON, CBOR, protocol buffers) by walking a five-step pipeline over the instance tree. Functor restrict handles tabular data (SQL) by precomposition along a schema morphism. Both are grounded in the same categorical idea—pulling data back along a map—but the concrete algorithms differ because trees and tables have different structure.
We will spend most of our time on the W-type pipeline, since it is the more intricate of the two and the one that handles the recursive, nested data formats that dominate modern protocol design.
To make the pipeline concrete, we need a schema that exercises every step. The ATProto threadViewPost type is ideal: a post that contains an array of replies, each of which is itself a post. The recursion means the instance is a tree of arbitrary depth.
Suppose version 1 of the schema looks like this (simplified):
And version 2 drops likeCount from postView and drops displayName from profileViewBasic. A migration \(m: S_2 \to S_1\) exists (both changes are field removals, which are always backward-compatible). The migration’s vertex map tells us which vertices in \(S_1\) survive in \(S_2\).
Now consider a concrete instance—a thread with two replies, one of which has a nested reply:
This is the instance we will push through the five-step pipeline.

title: 7  Lifting Data
text:
threadViewPost
├── post: postView
│   ├── uri: string
│   ├── author: profileViewBasic
│   │   ├── did: string
│   │   ├── handle: string
│   │   └── displayName: string
│   ├── text: string
│   └── likeCount: integer
└── replies: array<threadViewPost>


root (threadViewPost)
├── post (postView)
│   ├── uri = "at://did:plc:abc/post/1"
│   ├── author (profileViewBasic)
│   │   ├── did = "did:plc:abc"
│   │   ├── handle = "alice.bsky.social"
│   │   └── displayName = "Alice"
│   ├── text = "Hello world"
│   └── likeCount = 42
└── replies
    ├── reply-1 (threadViewPost)
    │   ├── post (postView)
    │   │   ├── uri = "at://did:plc:def/post/2"
    │   │   ├── author (profileViewBasic)
    │   │   │   ├── did = "did:plc:def"
    │   │   │   ├── handle = "bob.bsky.social"
    │   │   │   └── displayName = "Bob"
    │   │   ├── text = "Nice post"
    │   │   └── likeCount = 7
    │   └── replies
    │       └── reply-1-1 (threadViewPost)
    │           └── ...
    └── reply-2 (threadViewPost)
        └── ...

title: 7  Lifting Data
7  Lifting Data
text:
7.3 The pipeline in sequence
Let us trace our threadViewPost example through all five steps.
Input: An instance tree with nodes for threadViewPost, postView, profileViewBasic, uri, author, did, handle, displayName, text, likeCount, and replies, recursively nested to depth 3.
Step 1 — anchor_surviving: Mark likeCount and displayName nodes as pruned. All other nodes survive. In a thread with 3 posts, this marks 6 nodes for removal (one likeCount and one displayName per post).
Step 2 — reachable_from_root: BFS from root. All surviving nodes are reachable (the pruned nodes are leaves). No additional nodes are removed.
Step 3 — ancestor_contraction: No contraction needed. Every surviving node’s parent is also surviving.
Step 4 — resolve_edge: No re-parenting occurred, so no edges need resolution. All original edges between surviving nodes carry over directly.
Step 5 — reconstruct_fans: The schema is a tree, not a hypergraph. No fans to reconstruct.
Output: The original instance tree, minus the likeCount and displayName leaf nodes. Every other node, edge, and value is preserved exactly.
This is the common case for protocol evolution: field removal produces a simple, fast restriction that touches only the affected leaves.

title: 7  Lifting Data
7  Lifting Data
text:
7.5 Performance
The restrict pipeline targets >1 million records per second for simple projections (field additions and removals) running in WASM. Several design decisions make this possible.

7.5.1 Compiled migrations
When a migration is loaded, panproto pre-computes everything it can:

Surviving sets: The set of schema vertices that survive is computed once and stored as a bitset. Step 1 becomes a single bitset lookup per node.
Remap tables: Edge resolution (step 4) is pre-computed into a flat lookup table indexed by (source_anchor, child_anchor). No hash map lookups at runtime.
Fan maps: Fan reconstruction (step 5) is pre-computed into a mapping from old fan IDs to new fan IDs with pre-allocated child slots.

The migration specification is effectively compiled into a small program that the restrict pipeline executes.


7.5.2 Arena allocation
Instance trees are allocated in a bumpalo arena. This has two benefits:

Allocation is O(1): Bump allocation is a pointer increment. No free-list management, no fragmentation.
Deallocation is O(1): The entire arena is freed at once when the instance is no longer needed. No tree of destructors to walk.

For the restrict pipeline specifically, the output tree is allocated in a fresh arena. Surviving nodes are written sequentially into the output arena, giving excellent cache locality.


7.5.3 Zero-copy fast path
For the common case—no contraction (step 3 is a no-op), no fan reconstruction (step 5 is a no-op), and trivial edge resolution (step 4 is a no-op)—the pipeline reduces to:

Walk the input tree.
For each node, check the surviving bitset.
If surviving, copy the node to the output arena with a rewritten anchor field.

The node’s payload (the actual data—strings, integers, booleans) is not copied. Only the structural metadata (anchor, parent pointer, child list) is written. The payload is referenced by pointer into the input arena. This is safe because the input arena outlives the restrict operation.
In benchmarks on an M2 MacBook Air, this fast path processes ~3.2 million nodes per second for a threadViewPost-like schema with 8 fields per post. The WASM target is slightly slower (~1.8M nodes/sec) due to bounds checking and the lack of SIMD, but still well above the 1M target.


7.5.4 When it gets expensive
The pipeline slows down when:

Deep contraction chains (step 3): Walking up to find the nearest surviving ancestor is O(depth). For deeply nested schemas with many pruned intermediate levels, this can be significant. panproto mitigates this by pre-computing a “skip ancestor” table during migration compilation.
Ambiguous resolution (step 4): If the resolver table is large (many parallel edges in the schema), the lookup has higher constant factors. This is rare in practice.
Large fans (step 5): Reconstructing fans with hundreds of children (wide SQL tables) requires scanning and filtering each fan. The cost is linear in fan width.

For all three cases, the pre-computation done during migration compilation amortizes the per-instance cost.

title: 7  Lifting Data
text:
7.6 The view from 10,000 feet
The restrict operation is the point where theory meets bytes. The categorical framework—polynomial functors, W-types, pushout complements—is not an academic exercise. Each piece of theory corresponds to a concrete algorithmic step:








Theory
Algorithm
Purpose




Signature restriction (Abbott et al. 2005)
anchor_surviving
Decide which nodes to keep


Connected component
reachable_from_root
Remove disconnected survivors


Pushout complement (Ehrig et al. 2006)
ancestor_contraction
Re-parent orphaned nodes


Complement selection (Löwe 1993)
resolve_edge
Choose edge labels after contraction


Limit cone (Barr and Wells 1990)
reconstruct_fans
Reconstruct hyperedges


Precomposition (Spivak 2012)
\(\Delta_F\)
Restrict tabular instances



The W-type pipeline handles the case that matters most for modern protocols: recursive, tree-structured data encoded as JSON, CBOR, or protocol buffers. The functor restrict handles the SQL case. Together, they cover the data formats that panproto targets.
The algorithmic contribution of panproto is not any single step—each step is individually well-understood. The contribution is the pipeline: the specific sequence of steps, the compilation strategy that makes the common case fast, and the correctness argument that flows from the categorical semantics. Because each step has a precise algebraic meaning, the pipeline composes correctly by construction. There are no edge cases to discover by fuzzing (though we fuzz anyway). The edge cases are classified by the theory: they correspond to the conditions under which each step is non-trivial.
The next chapter shows how to run the pipeline in both directions—not just forward (source to view) but backward (view back to source)—using the lens framework. The complement captured during get is exactly the information discarded by the restrict pipeline, and put uses it to reconstruct the original instance.

title: 7  Lifting Data
text:
7.7 Further reading
The W-type perspective on inductive data types originates with Gambino and Hyland (2004), who showed that W-types in locally cartesian closed categories correspond to initial algebras of polynomial functors. The container / interaction structure perspective of Abbott et al. (2005) gives the fibrational view that panproto uses for signature restriction.
The graph transformation approach to steps 3 and 4 follows Ehrig et al. (2006), particularly the treatment of pushout complements in adhesive categories. The non-uniqueness of pushout complements in the presence of parallel edges was first identified by Löwe (1993) in the context of graph grammars. Drewes et al. (1997) extends this to hypergraph grammars, which is the foundation for step 5.
The functorial data migration framework (\(\Delta_F\), \(\Sigma_F\), \(\Pi_F\)) is due to Spivak (2012). The general theory of Kan extensions that underlies the adjoint triple is covered in Mac Lane (1998), Chapter X.






Abbott, Michael, Thorsten Altenkirch, and Neil Ghani. 2005. “Containers: Constructing Strictly Positive Types.” Theoretical Computer Science 342 (1): 3–27. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tcs.2005.06.002.


Barr, Michael, and Charles Wells. 1990. Category Theory for Computing Science. Prentice Hall.


Drewes, Frank, Hans-Jörg Kreowski, and Annegret Habel. 1997. “Hyperedge Replacement Graph Grammars.” In Handbook of Graph Grammars and Computing by Graph Transformation, edited by Gert Rozenberg, vol. 1, 1. World Scientific. https://doi.org/10.1142/9789812384720_0002.


Ehrig, Hartmut, Karsten Ehrig, Ulrike Prange, and Gabriele Taentzer. 2006. Fundamentals of Algebraic Graph Transformation. Monographs in Theoretical Computer Science. Springer. https://doi.org/10.1007/3-540-31188-2.


Gambino, Nicola, and J. Martin E. Hyland. 2004. “Wellfounded Trees and Dependent Polynomial Functors.” Types for Proofs and Programs (TYPES 2003), Lecture notes in computer science, vol. 3085: 210–25. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-540-24849-1_14.


Löwe, Michael. 1993. “Algebraic Approach to Single-Pushout Graph Transformation.” Theoretical Computer Science 109 (1–2): 181–224. https://doi.org/10.1016/0304-3975(93)90068-5.


Mac Lane, Saunders. 1998. Categories for the Working Mathematician. 2nd ed. Vol. 5. Graduate Texts in Mathematics. Springer. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-1-4757-4721-8.


Spivak, David I. 2012. “Functorial Data Migration.” Information and Computation 217: 31–51. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ic.2012.05.001.

title: 8  Lenses: Going Both Ways
text:
8.1 The get/put pattern
Every migration in panproto is bidirectional. When you project data from a source schema to a view schema, you also get a receipt—the complement—that records everything the projection discarded. Given the complement and a (possibly modified) view, you can reconstruct the source. This round-trip property is not an implementation convenience; it is a mathematical guarantee provided by the theory of lenses.
This chapter introduces asymmetric lenses, shows how panproto implements them, and explains the combinator API that lets you build migrations from small, composable pieces.
An asymmetric lens \(\ell: S \rightleftarrows V\) consists of two operations:
The operations satisfy two laws:

title: 8  Lenses: Going Both Ways
text:
get : S → (V, C) — Project the source \(S\) to a view \(V\), capturing the complement \(C\) (everything discarded).
put : (V, C) → S — Restore the source from a (possibly modified) view and the complement.



8.1.1 GetPut (round-tripping)
\[\mathtt{put}(\mathtt{get}(s)) = s\]
Getting and then putting back (without modifying the view) recovers the original source exactly. No information is lost.


8.1.2 PutGet (view consistency)
\[\pi_1(\mathtt{get}(\mathtt{put}(v, c))) = v\]
Putting a view \(v\) into the source and then getting it back returns \(v\). The view is faithfully represented in the reconstructed source.
Together, these laws say that get and put are mutual inverses up to the complement. The complement is the “slack” that allows the source to carry more information than the view without violating invertibility.






NoteWhy “lens”?



The name comes from the idea of focusing: a lens focuses on a part of a larger structure (the view), while keeping the rest (the complement) available for reconstruction. The metaphor is apt—like an optical lens, a panproto lens lets you see a specific slice of the data while the surrounding context remains intact but out of focus.

title: 8  Lenses: Going Both Ways
text:
8.2 Lenses in panproto
In panproto, get is the restrict pipeline from Chapter 7 plus complement capture. put is reconstruction from the complement. The connection is direct:

get runs the five-step W-type restrict pipeline (or functor restrict for SQL) and simultaneously records what was removed at each step.
put replays the removal in reverse: re-attach fans, re-resolve edges, re-expand contracted ancestors, re-attach unreachable nodes, and restore pruned anchors.
