The lens laws hold because the complement records exactly what get discarded—no more, no less. put has enough information to invert each step, and the sequential structure of the pipeline means the inversions compose correctly.

title: 8  Lenses: Going Both Ways
text:
8.3 The complement
The complement is the central data structure of the lens. It is a receipt for what get threw away, organized by pipeline step.
pub struct Complement {
    /// Nodes pruned in step 1 (anchor not surviving).
    /// Maps node index to the full node (anchor, payload, children).
    pub dropped_nodes: HashMap<u32, Node>,

    /// Arcs (edges) that connected pruned nodes to surviving nodes.
    /// Each entry is (parent_idx, child_idx, edge_label).
    pub dropped_arcs: Vec<(u32, u32, Edge)>,

    /// Edge labels chosen by the resolver in step 4.
    /// Maps (new_parent, child) to the original intermediate path.
    pub contraction_choices: HashMap<(u32, u32), Vec<VertexId>>,

    /// Original parent of each contracted node (before re-parenting).
    pub original_parent: HashMap<u32, u32>,

    /// Fan children pruned in step 5 (hypergraph schemas only).
    pub pruned_fan_children: HashMap<FanId, Vec<u32>>,
}
Each field corresponds to a pipeline step:








Complement field
Pipeline step
What it records




dropped_nodes
Step 1: anchor_surviving
Nodes whose anchor was pruned


dropped_arcs
Step 2: reachable_from_root
Edges to unreachable nodes


contraction_choices
Step 3–4: contraction + resolution
The intermediate path that was collapsed


original_parent
Step 3: ancestor_contraction
Original parent before re-parenting


pruned_fan_children
Step 5: reconstruct_fans
Fan children that were removed



The complement is the difference between the source and the view. It has a precise size: it is proportional to the amount of data that the migration discards. For a migration that only removes two leaf fields from a 10-field schema, the complement is roughly 20% of the source. For a migration that projects away 90% of the schema, the complement is large.






NoteThe complement is opaque



The complement should be treated as an opaque blob by application code. Its internal structure is an implementation detail of the pipeline. The only operation you should perform on a complement is passing it to put. Inspecting or modifying the complement voids the lens laws.

title: 8  Lenses: Going Both Ways
8  Lenses: Going Both Ways
text:
8.5 Cambria-style combinators
Building migrations by hand—specifying vertex maps, edge maps, and resolver tables—is precise but tedious. For common schema changes, panproto provides combinators: small, pre-built lenses that you compose into a pipeline.
The design follows Litt et al. (2022)’s Cambria system, adapted to panproto’s categorical framework. Each combinator is a well-behaved lens (satisfies GetPut and PutGet). Composition of well-behaved lenses is a well-behaved lens. So a pipeline of combinators automatically satisfies the lens laws.
const lens = pipeline([
  renameField('displayName', 'name'),
  addField('bio', 'string', ''),
  removeField('legacyField'),
]);

8.5.1 Available combinators








Combinator
Forward (get)
Backward (put)




renameField(old, new)
Rename field in output
Rename back


addField(name, default)
Add field with default value
Remove the field


removeField(name)
Remove the field
Restore from complement


wrapInObject(field, wrapper)
Nest field inside a new object
Unwrap


hoistField(path)
Move a nested field up one level
Move back down


coerceType(field, from, to)
Convert value (e.g., string → int)
Convert back



Each combinator handles its own complement. removeField stores the removed value in the complement. addField stores nothing (the default is known). renameField stores nothing (the mapping is invertible). wrapInObject stores the wrapper structure. And so on.


8.5.2 Composition
Combinators compose via pipeline():
const migration = pipeline(
  renameField("userName", "handle"),
  removeField("internalId"),
  addField("version", 2),
  hoistField("profile.displayName"),
);
The pipeline applies combinators left to right for get and right to left for put. The complement is a stack: each combinator pushes its complement fragment during get, and put pops them in reverse order.
This is function composition in the lens category. If \(\ell_1: S \rightleftarrows T\) and \(\ell_2: T \rightleftarrows V\), the composite \(\ell_2 \circ \ell_1: S \rightleftarrows V\) has:
\[\mathtt{get}_{2 \circ 1}(s) = \mathtt{get}_2(\pi_1(\mathtt{get}_1(s)))\] \[\mathtt{put}_{2 \circ 1}(v, (c_1, c_2)) = \mathtt{put}_1(\mathtt{put}_2(v, c_2), c_1)\]
The complement of the composite is the pair \((c_1, c_2)\)—the complements from each stage.

title: 8  Lenses: Going Both Ways
8  Lenses: Going Both Ways
text:
8.7 Complement serialization
The complement needs to be stored somewhere between get and put. panproto serializes it as CBOR (Concise Binary Object Representation), which is compact and fast to parse.
For a typical protocol evolution (removing a few fields from a 20-field schema), the serialized complement is a few hundred bytes per record. For bulk migrations, complements can be batched into a single CBOR array, compressed with zstd, and stored alongside the migrated data.
The storage strategy depends on the application:

Ephemeral complement: Store in memory during a request-response cycle. The complement lives only as long as the request.
Persistent complement: Store in a database column or object store. Enables deferred round-tripping: migrate data forward today, receive modifications over time, migrate back later.
Streaming complement: In a pipeline (Kafka, NATS), attach the complement as a message header. Downstream consumers can round-trip without access to the original source.







NoteComplement compression



Because the complement consists mostly of dropped field values—which are often repetitive (e.g., many records with likeCount = 0 or displayName = null)—it compresses extremely well. In benchmarks on ATProto data, zstd compression reduces complement size by 85–95%.

title: 8  Lenses: Going Both Ways
text:
8.8 Symmetric lenses
Asymmetric lenses assume one schema is a “view” of the other—a strict sub-structure. This works well for protocol evolution within a single protocol family (ATProto v1 to v2), where the new schema is typically a refinement of the old.
But what about cross-protocol migration? When translating between ATProto and ActivityPub, neither schema is a sub-structure of the other. ATProto has likeCount but not published; ActivityPub has published but not likeCount. An asymmetric lens in either direction would lose data.
Symmetric lenses model this situation. A symmetric lens between \(S_1\) and \(S_2\) is a span of asymmetric lenses:
\[S_1 \leftarrow C \rightarrow S_2\]
The apex \(C\) is a common “core” schema that both \(S_1\) and \(S_2\) project to. The left leg \(\ell_1: C \rightleftarrows S_1\) migrates between the core and \(S_1\). The right leg \(\ell_2: C \rightleftarrows S_2\) migrates between the core and \(S_2\). To translate from \(S_1\) to \(S_2\):

get along \(\ell_1\): project \(S_1\) data to the core \(C\), capturing complement \(c_1\).
put along \(\ell_2\): embed the core data into \(S_2\), using a default complement \(c_2\).

The complements \(c_1\) and \(c_2\) record what each protocol has that the other lacks. If you later need to translate back from \(S_2\) to \(S_1\), you use \(c_1\) to restore the ATProto-specific fields and \(c_2\) to discard the ActivityPub-specific ones.
This is the construction of Hofmann et al. (2011), who showed that symmetric lenses form a category where the morphisms are equivalence classes of spans. The equivalence relation identifies spans that differ only in the choice of core schema, which corresponds to the intuition that the core is an implementation detail—what matters is the round-trip behavior.

8.8.1 The core schema
Finding the right core schema is a design problem, not an algorithmic one. For ATProto-to-ActivityPub translation, the core might include:

Post text (both protocols have it)
Author identifier (both have it, with different formats)
Timestamp (both have it)

But not:

Like count (ATProto only)
Content warning (ActivityPub only, via summary field)

panproto provides tooling to compute a maximal core (the categorical pullback of the two schemas along their shared structure), but the result is often too large or too small for practical use. In practice, the core is hand-curated by someone who understands both protocols.






NoteSymmetric lenses and information loss



A symmetric lens always loses information in both directions. Translating from ATProto to ActivityPub loses likeCount; translating back loses published. The complements track what was lost so that round-trips within a single direction are lossless. But a full cycle \(S_1 \to S_2 \to S_1\) is only lossless if the core contains everything in \(S_1\)—which means \(S_1\) is a sub-structure of the core, and we are back to the asymmetric case.
This is a fundamental limitation, not a panproto limitation. No system can translate between genuinely different schemas without losing something.

title: 8  Lenses: Going Both Ways
text:
8.9 The categorical picture
For readers comfortable with category theory, here is the precise placement of panproto’s lenses in the literature.
An asymmetric lens \(\ell: S \rightleftarrows V\) is a morphism in the lens category \(\mathbf{Lens}\). Objects are schemas; morphisms are lens pairs \((get, put)\) satisfying GetPut and PutGet. Composition is sequential pipeline composition as described in Section 8.5. Identity is the trivial lens \((id, \pi_1)\).
This is the original definition of Foster et al. (2007), who introduced lenses for bidirectional programming in the context of the Harmony file synchronizer.
The delta lens generalization of Johnson and Rosebrugh (2013) replaces the concrete complement with an abstract notion of “update propagation.” A delta lens is a functor-cofunctor pair between categories of states and updates. This connects lenses to fibrations: the lens laws become the lifting conditions for a Grothendieck fibration.
Clarke (2020) unifies the picture by showing that internal lenses in a double category correspond to functors that are simultaneously cofunctors—the “functor-cofunctor” duality that delta lenses exhibit. panproto’s complement-based lenses are a concrete instance of this abstract framework, where the complement plays the role of the cartesian lifting.
The practical implication: panproto’s lens laws are not ad hoc conditions. They are instances of general categorical coherence conditions. Any construction that satisfies the categorical conditions automatically satisfies the lens laws. This is why combinator composition preserves well-behavedness: it is functorial composition in \(\mathbf{Lens}\).






NoteDo I need to know category theory?



No. The combinator API (Section 8.5) hides all of this. You compose combinators, and the lens laws hold by construction. The categorical framework is the reason the laws hold, but you do not need to understand the reason to use the result.
The category theory matters when you are extending panproto—adding new combinators, supporting new data formats, or proving properties of migrations. For day-to-day use, get, put, and pipeline are the entire API.

title: 8  Lenses: Going Both Ways
text:
8.10 Lenses and the restrict pipeline
The connection between this chapter and Chapter 7 is worth making explicit.
The restrict pipeline from Chapter 7 is get without complement capture. It is the forward direction of the lens. panproto implements get by running the restrict pipeline and simultaneously building the complement at each step:

Step 1 (anchor_surviving): Record each pruned node in dropped_nodes.
Step 2 (reachable_from_root): Record arcs to unreachable nodes in dropped_arcs.
Step 3 (ancestor_contraction): Record original parents in original_parent.
Step 4 (resolve_edge): Record intermediate paths in contraction_choices.
Step 5 (reconstruct_fans): Record pruned fan children in pruned_fan_children.

The complement is built during the forward pass, not after. This is important for performance: the restrict pipeline makes a single pass over the instance tree, and complement capture adds only constant overhead per node.
The put operation replays the steps in reverse, using the complement to undo each one. The result is a reconstructed source that agrees with the original on every field except those that were modified in the view.

title: 8  Lenses: Going Both Ways
text:
8.11 Further reading
The foundational paper on asymmetric lenses for bidirectional programming is Foster et al. (2007). The treatment there is in terms of string lenses (regular-expression-based transformations on strings), but the abstract framework applies directly to tree and graph transformations.
Hofmann et al. (2011) introduced symmetric lenses and the span construction. Their proof that symmetric lenses form a category resolved a long-standing question about how to compose bidirectional transformations when neither direction is “primary.”
Johnson and Rosebrugh (2013) generalized both asymmetric and symmetric lenses to the delta lens framework, connecting lenses to fibrations and indexed categories. This is the deepest theoretical treatment and the one that most directly connects to panproto’s categorical foundations.
Clarke (2020) provides the most recent synthesis, unifying lenses, functors, and cofunctors in the framework of double categories. The paper is demanding but rewarding for readers with a strong category theory background.
Litt et al. (2022) is the practical inspiration for panproto’s combinator API. Cambria demonstrated that lens combinators are a viable approach to schema evolution in real systems (specifically, collaborative editing with CRDTs). panproto extends Cambria’s approach from flat JSON schemas to recursive, polynomial-functor schemas.






Clarke, Bryce. 2020. “Internal Lenses as Functors and Cofunctors.” Applied Category Theory 2019, Electronic proceedings in theoretical computer science, vol. 323: 183–95. https://doi.org/10.4204/EPTCS.323.13.


Foster, J. Nathan, Michael B. Greenwald, Jonathan T. Moore, Benjamin C. Pierce, and Alan Schmitt. 2007. “Combinators for Bidirectional Tree Transformations: A Linguistic Approach to the View-Update Problem.” ACM Transactions on Programming Languages and Systems 29 (3): Article 17. https://doi.org/10.1145/1232420.1232424.


Hofmann, Martin, Benjamin C. Pierce, and Daniel Wagner. 2011. “Symmetric Lenses.” Proceedings of the 38th ACM SIGPLAN-SIGACT Symposium on Principles of Programming Languages (POPL 2011), 371–84. https://doi.org/10.1145/1926385.1926428.


Johnson, Michael, and Robert Rosebrugh. 2013. “Delta Lenses and Opfibrations.” Proceedings of the 2nd International Workshop on Bidirectional Transformations (BX 2013), Electronic communications of the EASST, vol. 57. https://doi.org/10.14279/tuj.eceasst.57.875.


Litt, Geoffrey, Martin Kleppmann, and Marc Shapiro. 2022. Project Cambria: Schema Evolution for CRDTs. Ink & Switch research essay. https://www.inkandswitch.com/cambria/.

title: 9  Breaking Changes
text:
9.1 The diff
Schema evolution is a fact of life. Protocols grow new fields, deprecate old ones, tighten constraints, and occasionally restructure entirely. The question is not whether a schema will change but how to classify the change: is it safe to deploy, or will it break existing consumers?
panproto computes a structural diff between two schemas and classifies each change against the protocol’s theory-derived rules. The result is a compatibility verdict: fully compatible, backward compatible, or breaking. This chapter explains the classification, walks through concrete examples, and shows how to integrate the check into CI.
panproto’s diff algorithm operates on the schema’s graph structure—vertices, edges, and their attributes—rather than on a textual representation. Two schemas that are textually different but structurally identical (e.g., fields listed in a different order) produce an empty diff.
The diff produces a list of change descriptors, each identifying:
The change descriptors are the input to the compatibility classifier.

title: 9  Breaking Changes
text:
const oldSchema = atproto.schema()
  .vertex('post', 'record', { nsid: 'app.bsky.feed.post' })
  .vertex('post:body', 'object')
  .vertex('post:body.text', 'string')
  .edge('post', 'post:body', 'record-schema')
  .edge('post:body', 'post:body.text', 'prop', { name: 'text' })
  .constraint('post:body.text', 'maxLength', '3000')
  .build();

const newSchema = atproto.schema()
  .vertex('post', 'record', { nsid: 'app.bsky.feed.post' })
  .vertex('post:body', 'object')
  .vertex('post:body.text', 'string')
  .vertex('post:body.tags', 'array')
  .edge('post', 'post:body', 'record-schema')
  .edge('post:body', 'post:body.text', 'prop', { name: 'text' })
  .edge('post:body', 'post:body.tags', 'prop', { name: 'tags' })
  .constraint('post:body.text', 'maxLength', '3000')
  .build();

const report = panproto.diff(oldSchema, newSchema);
// report.compatibility: 'fully-compatible'
// report.changes: [{ kind: 'vertex-added', id: 'post:body.tags' }, ...]


What changed: a vertex, an edge, a constraint, or a structural relationship.
How it changed: added, removed, modified, or moved.
Where it changed: the path from the root to the affected element.



9.1.1 Structural diffing vs. textual diffing
A textual diff (like git diff) operates on serialized representations and is sensitive to formatting, ordering, and encoding choices. A structural diff operates on the mathematical object (the labeled graph) and is invariant to these surface-level differences.
This matters because two schema files can look very different textually while representing the same graph. Reordering fields in a JSON Schema, renaming internal $defs, or switching between inline and referenced definitions can all produce large textual diffs with zero structural changes. panproto’s diff sees through these differences.






NoteCanonicalization



Before diffing, panproto canonicalizes both schemas: it resolves all references, normalizes naming, sorts vertices and edges by a deterministic order, and strips metadata that does not affect the schema’s mathematical structure. The diff operates on canonical forms, ensuring that equivalent schemas always produce an empty diff.

title: 9  Breaking Changes
text:
9.2 Compatibility levels
panproto classifies schema changes into three levels. The classification is theory-derived: it follows directly from the existence conditions for migrations (Chapter 6) rather than from ad hoc rules.

9.2.1 Fully compatible
Both forward and backward migrations exist.

Old data can be read by new code (forward migration: old schema → new schema).
New data can be read by old code (backward migration: new schema → old schema).

This is the gold standard. Deploying a fully compatible change requires no coordination: old and new consumers can coexist indefinitely.
Examples: Renaming a field (with a migration that maps the old name to the new name). Adding an optional field with a default. Reordering fields.


9.2.2 Backward compatible
A forward migration exists, but no backward migration.

Old data can be read by new code.
New data may not be readable by old code.

This is acceptable for rolling deployments where consumers update before producers, or where old consumers can gracefully handle unknown data (e.g., by ignoring unrecognized fields).
Examples: Removing a field (old data is fine without it; new data lacks what old code expects). Adding a required field (old data can be given a default; new data has a field old code does not know about).


9.2.3 Breaking
No valid forward migration exists.

Existing data cannot be transformed to conform to the new schema.

This is the red line. A breaking change means that some existing data will be rejected or misinterpreted by code targeting the new schema. Deploying a breaking change without a data migration plan will cause failures.
Examples: Tightening a constraint below existing values. Changing a field’s type incompatibly. Removing a vertex that other vertices depend on.






NoteThe classification is decidable



The compatibility classification reduces to checking migration existence in both directions. As established in Chapter 6, migration existence is decidable for the class of schemas that panproto supports. Therefore, the compatibility classification is also decidable. There is no approximation or heuristic involved: panproto gives a definitive answer.

title: 9  Breaking Changes
text:
9.3 The connection to existence conditions
The compatibility classifier does not maintain its own set of rules. It delegates entirely to the migration existence checker from Chapter 6.
Given schemas \(S_\text{old}\) and \(S_\text{new}\):

Check forward existence: Does a migration \(m: S_\text{old} \to S_\text{new}\) exist? If yes, old data can be transformed to the new schema.
Check backward existence: Does a migration \(m': S_\text{new} \to S_\text{old}\) exist? If yes, new data can be transformed to the old schema.

The classification follows:



Forward exists
Backward exists
Classification




Yes
Yes
Fully compatible


Yes
No
Backward compatible


No
Yes
Forward compatible only


No
No
Breaking



The “forward compatible only” case (new data can be read by old code, but old data cannot be read by new code) is theoretically possible but rare in practice. It would arise from changes like loosening a constraint: old code can handle the wider range, but new code might receive data that was valid under the old, tighter constraint. panproto reports this case but treats it as breaking for safety, since the typical deployment assumption is that old data must be readable by new code.






NoteWhy not just check the diff?



Some schema registries classify changes by pattern-matching on the diff: “adding a field is backward compatible,” “removing a field is breaking,” and so on. This works for simple cases but fails for composed changes. Adding an optional field and tightening a constraint on an unrelated field is not simply the conjunction of “backward compatible” and “breaking”—the interaction between changes can produce surprising results.
panproto avoids this problem by not classifying individual changes at all. It classifies the migration as a whole by checking existence. The diff is useful for reporting what changed (so humans can understand the result), but it does not drive the classification.

title: 9  Breaking Changes
text:
9.4 Examples
Let us walk through four concrete changes and see how panproto classifies them.

9.4.1 Example 1: Adding an optional field
Change: Add an optional labels field (type: array<string>, default: []) to postView.
Forward migration (\(S_\text{old} \to S_\text{new}\)): Exists. Old data lacks labels, but the default value [] fills it in. The migration adds a leaf node with the default value at every postView instance.
Backward migration (\(S_\text{new} \to S_\text{old}\)): Exists. New data has labels, which the backward migration discards (it is a field removal, which the restrict pipeline handles in step 1).
Classification: Fully compatible.
Both directions work. Old consumers ignore the new field (it is not in their schema). New consumers see the default value for old data. This is the most common kind of protocol evolution.
Schema diff:
  + postView.labels: array<string> (optional, default: [])

Compatibility: FULLY COMPATIBLE
  Forward migration:  ✓ exists (add default)
  Backward migration: ✓ exists (drop field)


9.4.2 Example 2: Removing a field
Change: Remove the likeCount field from postView.
Forward migration (\(S_\text{old} \to S_\text{new}\)): Exists. Old data has likeCount, which the forward migration discards. The restrict pipeline handles this in step 1.
Backward migration (\(S_\text{new} \to S_\text{old}\)): Does not exist. New data lacks likeCount, and the old schema requires it (it is not optional). There is no way to conjure a valid likeCount value from nothing.
Classification: Backward compatible.
Old data can be read by new code (just drop likeCount). But new data cannot be read by old code (the required field is missing). Consumers must be updated before producers stop sending likeCount.
Schema diff:
  - postView.likeCount: integer

Compatibility: BACKWARD COMPATIBLE
  Forward migration:  ✓ exists (drop field)
  Backward migration: ✗ no valid migration (required field missing)






NoteWhat if the removed field were optional?



If likeCount had been optional in the old schema, then the backward migration would exist: new data simply omits the field, which is valid because the old schema does not require it. The classification would be fully compatible.
This is why marking fields as optional from the start is good protocol hygiene. An optional field can be added or removed without breaking compatibility in either direction.




9.4.3 Example 3: Tightening a constraint
Change: Reduce the maximum length of postView.text from 3000 characters to 300 characters.
Forward migration (\(S_\text{old} \to S_\text{new}\)): Does not exist. Old data may contain text values up to 3000 characters, which violate the new 300-character limit. The migration would need to truncate text, but truncation is not a valid structural transformation—it changes the data’s meaning.
Backward migration (\(S_\text{new} \to S_\text{old}\)): Exists. New data has text values up to 300 characters, which satisfy the old 3000-character limit (300 ≤ 3000).
Classification: Breaking.
Existing data may violate the new constraint. There is no structural migration that can make 3000-character posts fit in 300 characters without semantic loss.
Schema diff:
  ~ postView.text: maxLength 3000 → 300

Compatibility: BREAKING
  Forward migration:  ✗ constraint violation (existing data may exceed new limit)
  Backward migration: ✓ exists (new constraint is stricter, old is satisfied)
This is a case where the diff alone might mislead. “Changing a maxLength” sounds like a minor modification. But the direction of the change matters: tightening is breaking, loosening is (backward) compatible.


9.4.4 Example 4: Changing a vertex kind
Change: Change postView.likeCount from integer to string.
Forward migration (\(S_\text{old} \to S_\text{new}\)): Does not exist. Old data has integer values (42), which are not valid strings in the target schema. While some type coercions are possible (integer to string via toString()), panproto does not perform semantic coercions during structural migration—the types must be structurally compatible.
Backward migration (\(S_\text{new} \to S_\text{old}\)): Does not exist. New data has string values ("42" or "many"), which are not valid integers.
Classification: Breaking.
Neither direction works. The types are structurally incompatible.
Schema diff:
  ~ postView.likeCount: integer → string

Compatibility: BREAKING
  Forward migration:  ✗ type mismatch (integer ≠ string)
  Backward migration: ✗ type mismatch (string ≠ integer)






NoteCoercions and combinators



If you want to change a type with a known coercion (e.g., integer → string via formatting), you can use the coerceType combinator from Section 8.5 to build a manual migration. The combinator includes both directions (format and parse), so it satisfies the lens laws. But panproto will not infer this coercion automatically—you must explicitly declare it. The compatibility checker reports the structural classification; manual migrations can override it.

title: 9  Breaking Changes
text:
9.5 Composed changes
Real schema evolutions rarely consist of a single change. A new version might simultaneously add fields, remove fields, tighten one constraint, and loosen another. The compatibility of the composed change is not the “worst” individual classification—it is determined by checking migration existence for the composed schemas.
Consider a version bump that:

Adds an optional labels field (fully compatible individually)
Removes likeCount (backward compatible individually)
Tightens text maxLength from 3000 to 300 (breaking individually)

The composed change is breaking, because the forward migration does not exist (the constraint tightening prevents it). But this is not simply because “one change is breaking.” It is because the existence checker, applied to the full old and new schemas, finds no valid forward migration.
Now consider a different composition:

Adds an optional labels field (fully compatible)
Removes likeCount (backward compatible)
Loosens text maxLength from 300 to 3000 (backward compatible: new constraint is weaker)

The composed change is backward compatible: the forward migration exists (drop likeCount, add labels with default, existing text satisfies the loosened constraint), but the backward migration does not (new data may have text up to 3000 characters, which exceeds the old 300-character limit).
The point: composition of compatibility levels is not a lattice operation. It depends on the specific changes and how they interact. Checking migration existence on the composed schemas is the only reliable method.

title: 9  Breaking Changes
text:
9.6 The diff report
panproto produces a human-readable diff report that combines the structural diff with the compatibility classification. The report is designed for code review: a developer should be able to read it and understand both what changed and whether it is safe.
panproto diff v1/schema.json v2/schema.json

Schema: app.bsky.feed.defs#threadViewPost

Changes:
  + postView.labels: array<string> (optional, default: [])
  - postView.likeCount: integer
  ~ postView.text: maxLength 300 → 3000
  + profileViewBasic.avatarUrl: string (optional)

Compatibility: BACKWARD COMPATIBLE

  Forward migration:  ✓ exists
    - labels: filled with default []
    - likeCount: dropped
    - text: constraint loosened (valid)
    - avatarUrl: filled with default null

  Backward migration: ✗ does not exist
    - likeCount: required field missing in new schema
    - text: new data may exceed old maxLength (3000 > 300)

Recommendation: Safe to deploy if consumers update before producers.
The report lists every change, explains why each direction does or does not work, and gives a deployment recommendation.

title: 9  Breaking Changes
text:
9.7 CI integration
Schema compatibility checks belong in CI, not in code review checklists. panproto provides a CLI command that exits with a non-zero status for breaking changes, making it easy to integrate into any CI system.

9.7.1 GitHub Actions
name: Schema Compatibility Check

on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - 'schemas/**'

jobs:
  check-compat:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0  # Need history to get the base schema

      - name: Install panproto
        run: cargo install panproto-cli

      - name: Get base schema
        run: git show origin/main:schemas/schema.json > /tmp/old-schema.json

      - name: Check compatibility
        run: |
          panproto diff /tmp/old-schema.json schemas/schema.json

      - name: Block breaking changes
        run: |
          panproto check \
            --src /tmp/old-schema.json \
            --tgt schemas/schema.json \
            --level backward-compatible
The panproto check command accepts a --level flag:

--level fully-compatible: Fail unless the change is fully compatible in both directions.
--level backward-compatible: Fail unless at least a forward migration exists (the default).
--level breaking: Never fail (only report). Useful for auditing.



9.7.2 CLI usage
For local development, the same commands work without CI:
# Show the diff and classification
panproto diff old-schema.json new-schema.json

# Check against a specific compatibility level (exit code 0 = pass, 1 = fail)
panproto check --src old-schema.json --tgt new-schema.json --level backward-compatible

# Output as JSON for programmatic consumption
panproto diff old-schema.json new-schema.json --format json


9.7.3 Schema registry integration
For teams that use a schema registry, panproto can check compatibility against the latest registered version:
# Check against the latest version in the registry
panproto check \
  --src "registry://app.bsky.feed.defs#threadViewPost@latest" \
  --tgt schemas/schema.json
This integrates with the registry’s version history, so you always check against what is actually deployed rather than what is on the main branch.






NoteBlocking vs. warning



Not every team wants to block on backward compatibility. Some protocols (internal APIs with synchronized deployments) can tolerate breaking changes as long as they are intentional. panproto supports this with the --level flag: set it to breaking to get reports without blocking, or add an --allow-breaking flag to specific PRs via a label-based CI override.
The important thing is that the check runs and the result is visible. Even if you do not block, knowing that a change is breaking before it ships is valuable.

title: 9  Breaking Changes
text:
9.8 The graph-theoretic foundation
The compatibility classification rests on two theoretical results.

9.8.1 Existence as a graph property
A migration from \(S_\text{old}\) to \(S_\text{new}\) exists if and only if certain graph-theoretic conditions are satisfied: the vertex map must be a valid graph homomorphism, the edge map must respect the boundary operators, and the constraint maps must be monotone in the appropriate direction. These conditions are decidable for finite graphs, which is why panproto can give definitive compatibility verdicts (Ehrig et al. 2006).
The key insight from algebraic graph transformation theory is that the existence of a pushout complement (which underlies the restrict pipeline’s step 3) can be characterized purely in terms of the gluing condition: a condition on the interface between the part being removed and the part being kept. When the gluing condition is satisfied, the pushout complement exists and is unique (for adhesive categories). When it is not satisfied, no valid migration exists.


9.8.2 Adhesive categories and decidability
The schemas that panproto supports form an adhesive category (Lack and Sobociński 2005). In an adhesive category, pushouts along monomorphisms exist and have good properties: they are stable under pullback, and van Kampen squares exist. The practical consequence is that the existence of pushout complements—and therefore the existence of migrations—is decidable by checking a finite set of local conditions.
This is not true for arbitrary categories. In a general category, checking whether a pushout complement exists can be undecidable. The restriction to adhesive categories is what makes panproto’s compatibility checker sound and complete: it never gives a false positive (declaring a change compatible when it is not) and never gives a false negative (declaring a change breaking when a valid migration exists).






NoteSoundness and completeness



Sound: If panproto says a forward migration exists, it does. You can trust a “fully compatible” or “backward compatible” verdict.
Complete: If panproto says no migration exists, none does. You cannot work around a “breaking” verdict by being clever with the migration specification (though you can use manual combinators to define a non-structural migration that panproto does not reason about).
Soundness and completeness are properties of the existence checker, not of the diff. The diff is a reporting tool; the existence checker is the decision procedure.

title: 9  Breaking Changes
text:
9.9 Versioning strategies
The compatibility classification informs but does not dictate versioning strategy. Different protocols adopt different conventions.

9.9.1 Semantic versioning
Map compatibility levels to semver:

Fully compatible → patch or minor version bump
Backward compatible → minor version bump
Breaking → major version bump

This is the most common approach and works well for protocols with external consumers who cannot be forced to update.


9.9.2 Chronological versioning
Some protocols (ATProto’s Lexicon) use date-based or sequential version numbers without semver semantics. The compatibility level is still useful for deployment decisions, but it does not drive the version number.


9.9.3 Continuous evolution
Some internal protocols avoid versioning entirely, relying on the lens mechanism (Chapter 8) to keep old and new consumers working simultaneously. In this model, every change is deployed with its migration, and the compatibility level determines whether old consumers need any changes:

Fully compatible: Deploy freely.
Backward compatible: Deploy new consumers first, then new producers.
Breaking: Deploy a data migration, then new consumers, then new producers.

The compatibility checker ensures that the deployment plan matches the actual compatibility of the change.

title: 9  Breaking Changes
text:
9.10 Putting it all together
The breaking changes workflow in panproto connects every concept from the preceding chapters:

Schemas (Chapters 1–2) define the structure being evolved.
Theories (Chapters 3–4) constrain which evolutions are valid.
Existence conditions (Chapter 6) determine whether a migration is possible.
The restrict pipeline (Chapter 7) implements the forward migration.
Lenses (Chapter 8) make the migration bidirectional.
The compatibility classifier (this chapter) decides whether the change is safe to deploy.

The classifier is the user-facing endpoint of the theory. A developer does not need to understand polynomial functors or pushout complements to use panproto check. They see a clear verdict—fully compatible, backward compatible, or breaking—backed by a proof-strength guarantee from the underlying mathematics.
This is the design philosophy of panproto: the theory is deep, but the interface is simple. The complexity lives in the library, not in the developer’s head.

title: 9  Breaking Changes
text:
9.11 Further reading
The graph-theoretic foundation for schema compatibility draws on Ehrig et al. (2006), particularly the treatment of pushout complements and the gluing condition in the context of typed attributed graph grammars. The decidability of the compatibility classification relies on the adhesive category framework of Lack and Sobociński (2005), which provides the categorical conditions under which pushout complement existence is decidable.
For the practical aspects of schema compatibility checking in production systems, the Confluent Schema Registry documentation provides useful context on how compatibility levels are used in event-streaming architectures. panproto’s classification is more fine-grained (because it reasons about structural migrations rather than syntactic patterns), but the deployment strategies are similar.






Ehrig, Hartmut, Karsten Ehrig, Ulrike Prange, and Gabriele Taentzer. 2006. Fundamentals of Algebraic Graph Transformation. Monographs in Theoretical Computer Science. Springer. https://doi.org/10.1007/3-540-31188-2.


Lack, Stephen, and Paweł Sobociński. 2005. “Adhesive and Quasiadhesive Categories.” RAIRO – Theoretical Informatics and Applications 39 (3): 511–45. https://doi.org/10.1051/ita:2005028.

title: 10  Building Your Own Protocol
text:
10.1 Colimits (Pushouts) of Theories
So far we have worked with protocols that panproto ships out of the box—76 protocols across 9 categories—serialization, data schemas, APIs, databases, type systems, web/document formats, data science, domain-specific, and configuration. But panproto is not a fixed catalog. It is a toolkit for building schema languages, and the mechanism for doing so is the colimit of theories.
This chapter shows how to define a new protocol from scratch by composing reusable component theories, and how the full machinery—migrations, existence checking, diff, lifting—comes along for free.
A colimit is the categorical way to glue two structures together along a shared part. In panproto, the structures are GAT theories and the shared part is a smaller theory that both include.
Concretely, given two theories \(T_1\) and \(T_2\) that each contain (via theory morphisms) a common sub-theory \(S\), the colimit \(T_1 +_S T_2\) produces a new theory that:
The Rust API exposes this as a single function:

title: 10  Building Your Own Protocol
text:
Contains all sorts and operations from both \(T_1\) and \(T_2\).
Identifies anything that came from the shared base \(S\), so nothing is duplicated.
Preserves all equations from both sides.


pub fn colimit(t1: &Theory, t2: &Theory, shared: &Theory) -> Result<Theory, GatError>;

10.1.1 A Concrete Example
Consider two theories:

ThGraph with sorts {Vertex, Edge} and operations {src: Edge → Vertex, tgt: Edge → Vertex}.
ThConstraint with sorts {Vertex, Constraint} and operations {target: Constraint → Vertex}.

Both theories contain the sort Vertex. The shared base ThVertex is simply the theory with the single sort {Vertex} and no operations.
The colimit ThConstrainedGraph = colimit(ThGraph, ThConstraint, ThVertex) merges them:

Sorts: {Vertex, Edge, Constraint} — Vertex appears once, not twice.
Operations: {src, tgt, target} — all operations are retained.







graph LR
    ThVertex -->|includes Vertex| ThGraph
    ThVertex -->|includes Vertex| ThConstraint
    ThGraph -->|colimit leg| ThConstrainedGraph
    ThConstraint -->|colimit leg| ThConstrainedGraph




Figure 10.1: Pushout square: ThGraph and ThConstraint glued along their shared ThVertex.





The colimit legs are theory morphisms from each component into the result. These morphisms are what let panproto track the provenance of each sort and operation—essential for computing migrations later.






Note



Colimits of GATs are computed by taking a coequalizer in the category of presentations. The implementation follows the algorithm described in Lynch et al. (2024), which handles the subtleties of dependent sorts and parametric operations that arise in full GATs (not just single-sorted algebraic theories).

title: 10  Building Your Own Protocol
10  Building Your Own Protocol
text:
10.3 Theory Library
panproto ships with a library of reusable component theories designed to be composed via colimits:



Theory
What it provides




ThGraph
Vertices and directed edges (src, tgt)


ThConstraint
Constraint annotations on vertices


ThMulti
Multiple edge types between the same vertices


ThHypergraph
Hyperedges connecting arbitrary sets of vertices


ThInterface
Input/output interface boundaries


ThWType
W-type structure for recursive/inductive data


ThFunctor
Functorial mappings between graph-like structures


ThMeta
Meta-level annotations (documentation, deprecation)


ThFlat
Flat key-value records (no nesting)



Every protocol panproto ships is defined as a colimit of theories from this library. The library is not closed; you can define your own component theories and use them in colimits alongside the built-in ones.






Note



The compositional nature of colimits means that if two protocols share a component theory, panproto can automatically construct a partial translation between them. This is the foundation for cross-protocol migration, which we touched on in Chapter 5 and which becomes fully systematic once you see protocols as colimits.



10.3.1 All Protocols as Colimits
Every one of panproto’s 76 built-in protocols falls into one of six theory groups, determined by which component theories its colimit requires. The table below summarizes the groups; the instance theory column names the theory used to represent data (instances) for schemas in that group.



Table 10.1: The six theory groups organising panproto’s 76 built-in protocols.

Group membership determines which generic algorithms apply and how cross-group migrations are constructed—for instance, migrating from a Group C protocol (Protobuf) to a Group A protocol (JSON Schema) requires a theory morphism that embeds the simpler ThSimpleGraph into the richer colimit(ThGraph, ThConstraint, ThMulti). The migration morphism itself remains the same kind of graph homomorphism described throughout this tutorial.
See Chapter 18 for the complete catalog of all 76 protocols with their theory group assignments.

title: 10  Building Your Own Protocol
text:
10.4 Summary
Defining a new protocol in panproto is not an act of implementing a plugin or writing an adapter. It is an act of specifying a theory, typically by composing existing theories via colimits. The colimit merges shared structure, the theory morphisms track provenance, and the generic engine provides migrations, diffs, existence checking, and lifting—all derived from the mathematics.
This is what it means for panproto to be protocol-parametric: the protocol is a parameter, and changing it requires no changes to the engine.

10.4.1 Further Reading
The theory of colimits in the context of generalized algebraic theories is developed in Cartmell (1986). The computational algorithm for colimits of GAT presentations, which panproto’s implementation follows, is described in Lynch et al. (2024).






Cartmell, John. 1986. “Generalised Algebraic Theories and Contextual Categories.” Annals of Pure and Applied Logic 32: 209–43. https://doi.org/10.1016/0168-0072(86)90053-9.


Lynch, Owen, Kris Brown, James Fairbanks, and Evan Patterson. 2024. “GATlab: Modeling and Programming with Generalized Algebraic Theories.” arXiv Preprint arXiv:2404.04837, ahead of print. https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2404.04837.

title: 11  Self-Description: The GAT of GATs
text:
11.1 Four Levels
panproto’s architecture has a recursive quality that is easy to overlook on first encounter but turns out to be one of its most consequential features. The system that describes schemas is itself described by a schema. The theory language is itself a theory.
This chapter makes that precise and explains why it matters.
panproto organizes its abstractions into four levels. Each level is defined by the one below it.
graph TB
    L0["Level 0: GAT Engine<br/>(hardcoded Rust, ~2000 lines)"]
    L1["Level 1: Theory Specifications<br/>(GAT data: ThGraph, ThConstraint, …)"]
    L2["Level 2: Concrete Schemas<br/>(models of Level 1 theories)"]
    L3["Level 3: Concrete Instances<br/>(models of Level 2 schemas)"]
    L0 -->|interprets| L1
    L1 -->|parameterizes| L2
    L2 -->|governs| L3




Figure 11.1: The four levels of panproto’s self-description hierarchy.
crumbs: 11  Self-Description: The GAT of GATs

title: 11  Self-Description: The GAT of GATs
text:
11.1.1 Level 0: The GAT Engine
The bottom of the stack is approximately 2000 lines of Rust that implement the core GAT machinery: sorts, operations, equations, theory morphisms, colimits, and model checking. This is the only part of panproto that is not data-driven. It is the interpreter for everything above.


11.1.2 Level 1: Theory Specifications
The theories we have been working with throughout this tutorial—ThGraph, ThConstraint, ThConstrainedMultiGraph, and the protocol-specific theories for ATProto, SQL, and so on—are all data at this level. They are values of type Theory, constructed by the Level 0 engine. Adding a new schema language means adding new Level 1 data; no Rust code changes.


11.1.3 Level 2: Concrete Schemas
A schema is a model of a Level 1 theory. When you write an ATProto lexicon, you are constructing a model of ThConstrainedMultiGraph. When you write a SQL DDL, you are constructing a model of the SQL protocol theory. The schema is governed by the theory it instantiates, in the same way that a value is governed by its type.


11.1.4 Level 3: Concrete Instances
The actual data—a Bluesky post, a row in a database, a Protobuf message—lives at Level 3. An instance is a model of a Level 2 schema. It is the concrete content that conforms to a specific schema within a specific protocol.






Note



Migrations happen at every level, using the same engine:

Level 1 to Level 1: A theory morphism transforms one schema language into another.
Level 2 to Level 2: A schema morphism (a model homomorphism) transforms one schema into another within the same protocol.
Level 3 to Level 3: Data migration via restrict transforms instances to conform to a new schema.

The machinery is uniform. The same lift, get, put, and checkExistence operations work at each level.
crumbs: 11  Self-Description: The GAT of GATs

title: 11  Self-Description: The GAT of GATs
crumbs: 11  Self-Description: The GAT of GATs

title: 11  Self-Description: The GAT of GATs
text:
11.3 Why Self-Description Matters
Self-description is not a curiosity. It has three practical consequences.

11.3.1 Versioning the Theory Language
If you need to extend what a “sort” or “operation” can express—say, adding support for dependent sorts with multiple parameters, or adding a new kind of equation—you can define a new version of ThGAT and construct a theory morphism from the old version to the new one. The migration engine then handles the upgrade of all Level 1 data (your theory specifications) automatically.
Without self-description, extending the theory language would require rewriting the Level 0 engine and manually migrating all existing theory definitions. With it, the extension is a data migration like any other.


11.3.2 Uniform Tooling
Because theories are models of ThGAT, all the tooling that operates on models—diff, existence checking, lifting, lenses—can be applied to theories themselves. You can diff two versions of a protocol’s theory, check whether a migration between them exists, and lift schemas through the change. The tooling is not specialized per level; it is generic and uniform.


11.3.3 Formal Guarantees at Every Level
The correctness properties that panproto provides—round-tripping for lenses, completeness of existence checking, functoriality of migrations—hold at every level of the hierarchy. A theory morphism between Level 1 theories satisfies the same laws as a schema morphism between Level 2 schemas. This uniformity is a direct consequence of the self-description architecture: every level is an instance of the same mathematical framework.
crumbs: 11  Self-Description: The GAT of GATs

title: 11  Self-Description: The GAT of GATs
text:
11.4 The System is Closed
There is an appealing sense in which panproto is closed. The only code that cannot be replaced by data is Level 0—the GAT engine itself, which interprets everything above it. Everything at Level 1 and above is defined by the level below, and can be extended, versioned, and migrated using the same engine.
This is the sense in which panproto is universal. It is not universal because it supports every protocol (it does not, yet). It is universal because the mechanism for adding a new protocol is the same mechanism that the existing protocols use, and that mechanism is itself described within the system.
The GAT engine is small, fixed, and mathematically well-founded. Everything built on top of it inherits those properties. This is the architectural bet at the heart of panproto: a small, principled foundation can support an open-ended space of schema languages, because the foundation is expressive enough to describe itself.

11.4.1 Further Reading
The idea that a type theory can describe its own types goes back to Cartmell (1986)’s contextual categories, where the category of contexts is itself a contextual category. Lynch et al. (2024) describes the self-description architecture of GATlab, which panproto’s design follows closely. Schultz et al. (2017) explores a partial version of this idea in the CQL system, where the “typeside” plays a role analogous to panproto’s Level 0 but without full self-description.






Cartmell, John. 1986. “Generalised Algebraic Theories and Contextual Categories.” Annals of Pure and Applied Logic 32: 209–43. https://doi.org/10.1016/0168-0072(86)90053-9.


Lynch, Owen, Kris Brown, James Fairbanks, and Evan Patterson. 2024. “GATlab: Modeling and Programming with Generalized Algebraic Theories.” arXiv Preprint arXiv:2404.04837, ahead of print. https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2404.04837.


Schultz, Patrick, David I. Spivak, Christina Vasilakopoulou, and Ryan Wisnesky. 2017. “Algebraic Databases.” Theory and Applications of Categories 32 (16): 547–619. http://www.tac.mta.ca/tac/volumes/32/16/32-16abs.html.

title: 12  Format Conversion
text:
13 Format Conversion
There is a program called pandoc that converts between document formats. You hand it a Markdown file and it gives you a PDF, an HTML page, a Word document, or a LaTeX source file. The conversion is not lossless—some Markdown constructs have no meaningful representation in Word, and some Word features have no Markdown equivalent—but for ordinary prose, pandoc gets you close enough that the conversion is practically useful.
panproto is pandoc for schemas.
Every protocol in panproto ships with both a parse_* function that reads a native schema document into the universal Schema graph and an emit_* function that writes a Schema graph back out to a native schema document. These two functions together enable a format conversion pipeline: parse from format A, obtain a universal intermediate representation, optionally migrate or transform that representation, and emit to format B.
This chapter walks through what that pipeline looks like in practice, what it can and cannot faithfully carry across format boundaries, and why some cross-format conversions are structurally more faithful than others.

title: 12  Format Conversion
section: 13.1 A Motivating Example: Avro to Protobuf

title: 12  Format Conversion
section: 13.2 The Parse → Schema → Migrate → Emit Pipeline
text:
13.2 The Parse → Schema → Migrate → Emit Pipeline
The general pipeline has four stages:






graph LR
    A["Source document<br/>(format A)"]
    B["Schema graph<br/>(universal IR)"]
    C["Schema graph<br/>(optionally transformed)"]
    D["Target document<br/>(format B)"]

    A -->|"parse_*()"| B
    B -->|"migrate / transform"| C
    C -->|"emit_*()"| D




Figure 13.1: The format conversion pipeline. Every protocol has both a parse and an emit function. The Schema graph is the universal intermediate representation.





Parse. The parse_* function for a protocol reads its native schema notation and constructs a Schema graph. The parse step is responsible for understanding protocol-specific syntax and encoding it into the protocol-neutral graph representation. What cannot be represented in the graph is dropped or converted to a best-effort annotation.
Schema graph. The universal intermediate representation is the same Schema graph that panproto uses for everything else—diffs, migrations, lens derivation, breaking-change detection. It is not a separate format invented for conversion; it is the standard representation, which is why the rest of the panproto machinery can be applied to it between the parse and emit steps.
Migrate / transform. This stage is optional. You can apply a panproto migration to the Schema graph between parse and emit—for example, to rename a field, flatten a nested type, or restructure a hierarchy. This is the stage where the cross-format conversion acquires the full power of panproto’s migration engine. Without this stage, the pipeline is a direct format translation; with it, the pipeline can simultaneously translate and transform.
Emit. The emit_* function for a protocol walks the Schema graph and produces native schema notation. The emit step is responsible for mapping the protocol-neutral representation back to the target format’s specific constructs—assigning field numbers, choosing appropriate type names, generating the correct syntax.
Every one of panproto’s 76 built-in protocols has both a parse and an emit function. This means every pair of protocols is connected by a potential conversion path through the shared Schema graph.

title: 12  Format Conversion
12  Format Conversion
text:
13.4 Round-Trip Fidelity
Format conversion is not lossless in general. What survives a round trip—parse from format A, emit to format B, parse from format B, emit back to format A—depends on how much structural territory the two formats share.
There are two sources of information loss:
Parse loss. When parse_* reads a source document, it must represent the source format’s constructs in the universal Schema graph. Constructs that have no graph-level equivalent are dropped. SQL foreign keys that reference three tables simultaneously (a hyperedge) can be represented in the Schema graph, but a CHECK constraint containing an arbitrary SQL expression may be captured only as an opaque annotation—its semantics are not machine-processable at the schema level.
Emit loss. When emit_* writes a target document, it must represent the Schema graph’s constructs in the target format’s notation. Constructs in the Schema graph that have no target-format equivalent are dropped or approximated. Hyperedges are dropped when emitting to a format with no hyperedge concept. Constraint keywords are dropped when emitting to a format with no constraint vocabulary.
A useful heuristic is to ask whether the source and target formats are structurally comparable:








Conversion
What survives
What is typically lost




JSON Schema → TypeScript
Field names, types, optionality
Constraint keywords (maxLength, pattern)


SQL DDL → Parquet
Column names, types, nullability
Foreign keys, CHECK constraints, defaults


GraphQL SDL → OpenAPI
Type names, field names, types, interfaces
Directives, resolver-level semantics


Protobuf → FlatBuffers
Message names, field names, types
Field numbers, service definitions


Avro → Protobuf
Record names, field names, types
Logical types (approximated), union semantics



The middle column—what survives—corresponds to the structural skeleton of the schema: the graph of types and relationships. This is what the Schema graph represents faithfully. The right column—what is lost—corresponds to format-specific decorations and semantic extensions that lie outside the schema graph’s representational scope.

title: 12  Format Conversion
text:
13.5 The Theory Group Advantage
The heuristic of “structural comparability” has a precise underpinning in panproto’s architecture. As established in Chapter 4, every protocol is characterized by a pair of theories: a schema theory and an instance theory. Protocols whose schema theories are closely related will have higher-fidelity conversions.
panproto’s built-in protocols cluster into five theory groups based on their schema and instance theories:



Table 13.1: Theory groups for selected protocols. Protocols in the same group share the same schema and instance theories, enabling structurally lossless conversion.











Group
Schema theory family
Instance theory
Representative protocols




A
ThCategory
ThFunctor
CQL


B
ThHypergraph + ThConstraint
ThFunctor
SQL DDL, Cassandra, DynamoDB


C
ThGraph + ThConstraint + ThMulti
ThWType
JSON Schema, ATProto, Avro, Protobuf, Thrift, MsgPack, Cap’n Proto, FlatBuffers


D
ThGraph + ThConstraint + ThMulti + ThInterface
ThWType
GraphQL SDL, OpenAPI, AsyncAPI


E
ThGraph + ThConstraint + ThMulti
ThFunctor
Parquet, Arrow, DataFrame






The table is simplified—actual protocols add additional component theories for protocol-specific constructs such as field numbering (Protobuf), grapheme limits (ATProto), or service definitions (gRPC). But the dominant theory combination, which determines the structural shape of schemas and the shape of data, follows this grouping.
Within-group conversion is structurally lossless at the schema level. Two Group C protocols—say, Avro and Protobuf—share the same schema theory: colimit(ThGraph, ThConstraint, ThMulti). A Schema graph that is a valid Avro schema is, structurally, also a valid Protobuf schema. The conversion between them reduces to remapping protocol-specific surface syntax: Avro’s "type": "record" becomes Protobuf’s message, Avro’s "type": ["null", "string"] becomes Protobuf’s optional string. The graph structure itself does not change.
Cross-group conversion involves a structural mismatch. Converting from Group B (SQL, hypergraph + functor) to Group C (Protobuf, multigraph + W-type) loses hyperedge structure: foreign keys, composite unique constraints, and multi-column primary keys cannot be expressed in Group C’s multigraph schema theory. Converting from Group C to Group B in the other direction loses tree-shaped instance semantics: SQL rows are flat, and the nested document structure of a Group C instance does not survive the trip to a relational table without denormalization decisions that the engine cannot make automatically.






graph LR
    A["Group A<br/>CQL"]
    B["Group B<br/>SQL, Cassandra, DynamoDB"]
    C["Group C<br/>JSON Schema, Avro,<br/>Protobuf, FlatBuffers"]
    D["Group D<br/>GraphQL, OpenAPI,<br/>AsyncAPI"]
    E["Group E<br/>Parquet, Arrow"]

    C <-->|lossless| C
    D <-->|lossless| D
    B <-->|lossless| B

    C <-.->|"structural loss<br/>(hyperedges → dropped)"| B
    C <-.->|"structural loss<br/>(no interfaces)"| D
    D <-.->|"structural loss<br/>(hyperedges → dropped)"| B
    B <-.->|"structural loss<br/>(functor/W-type mismatch)"| E
    C <-.->|"structural loss<br/>(instance theory mismatch)"| E




Figure 13.2: Theory groups and conversion fidelity. Solid arrows indicate structurally lossless conversion (within-group). Dashed arrows indicate conversion with structural loss (cross-group).





The diagram shows that cross-group conversions always carry some structural loss. Within-group conversions—the solid double-headed arrows—are structurally lossless: the schema graph is preserved intact, and only surface syntax changes.

13.5.1 A Note on “Lossless”
“Structurally lossless at the schema level” means the graph structure of the schema survives the round trip. It does not mean the round trip produces identical source bytes, or that all protocol-specific metadata is preserved. Protobuf field numbers, for example, are not part of the multigraph structure—they are a surface feature of the wire format. A Protobuf-to-Avro-to-Protobuf round trip will not preserve field numbers; the emit step will assign fresh ones. The schema graph—the message names, field names, types, and relationships—will be intact, but the wire-format metadata will not.
If your conversion requirements include preserving wire-format metadata, you can carry it as an opaque annotation through the Schema graph. panproto’s Schema graph supports arbitrary key-value metadata on vertices and edges; the parse functions use this to preserve information that is meaningful to the source format but has no universal semantic. The emit function for the target format can then ignore this metadata, or a custom emit wrapper can read it and apply it selectively.

title: 12  Format Conversion
text:
13.6 Comparison with pandoc
The pandoc analogy that opened this chapter is worth examining in more detail, because the structural parallel is exact in some places and breaks down in others.
What is the same. Both pandoc and panproto convert between multiple formats using a shared intermediate representation. pandoc’s IR is a document abstract syntax tree: a tree of Pandoc elements (paragraphs, headers, code blocks, spans, and so on). panproto’s IR is the Schema graph: a multigraph or hypergraph with constraint annotations. In both systems, the IR is the canonical representation, and all conversion goes through it. Neither system converts directly between pairs of formats; all paths go through the IR.
Both systems also face the same fundamental limitation: information that cannot be represented in the IR is lost. pandoc cannot round-trip Word documents with custom styles, tracked changes, or embedded macros, because these features have no equivalent in the Pandoc AST. panproto cannot round-trip SQL schemas with arbitrary CHECK expressions, because these expressions are opaque at the schema graph level.
What is different. pandoc’s IR is a fixed, hand-designed document AST. Adding a new element type to the AST requires changing pandoc’s source code. panproto’s IR is not fixed—it is the Schema graph, whose structure is determined by the protocol theories. Adding a new structural concept (a new sort of vertex or edge) means extending a theory via a colimit, not patching the IR definition.
This difference matters for extensibility. When a new schema format appears—say, a domain-specific schema language for a new database engine—panproto can accommodate it by defining a new protocol theory. The Schema graph automatically gains the new format’s structural concepts as a natural extension of the theory. pandoc, by contrast, would require an AST extension to handle document constructs with no existing element type.
The second difference is in the use of the IR. In pandoc, the IR exists solely for conversion; you interact with it by writing filters that transform the AST. In panproto, the Schema graph is the primary representation for all operations: diff, migration, existence checking, lens derivation, breaking-change detection, and conversion all operate on the same Schema graph. Format conversion is not a separate subsystem—it is the composition of parse_* and emit_* applied to the same object that the rest of the toolchain already uses.



Table 13.2: Structural comparison between pandoc and panproto.











pandoc
panproto




Converts between
Document formats
Schema formats


Intermediate representation
Pandoc document AST
Schema graph


IR defined by
Fixed Haskell data types
Protocol theories (extensible)


IR used for
Conversion only
Conversion, diff, migration, lenses, …


Loss mechanism
AST has no element for X
Schema graph has no vertex/edge for X


Format-specific extensions
Custom AST metadata
Theory-specific constraint annotations

title: 12  Format Conversion
text:
13.7 Summary

panproto is to schemas what pandoc is to documents: it converts between formats using a universal intermediate representation, the Schema graph.
Every protocol ships with a parse_* function (source format → Schema graph) and an emit_* function (Schema graph → target format). All 76 built-in protocols support both directions.
The conversion pipeline is: parse_*() → Schema graph → (optional migration) → emit_*(). The optional migration step allows simultaneous format conversion and structural transformation.
Round-trip fidelity depends on what the source and target formats share structurally. Information that fits in the Schema graph survives; information that does not—hyperedges in a format without hyperedge support, constraint keywords in a format without a constraint vocabulary—is dropped.
Theory groups make fidelity precise. Protocols sharing the same schema and instance theory (same group) convert with structural losslessness. Cross-group conversions involve structural mismatches: Group B (SQL, hypergraph) to Group C (Protobuf, multigraph) loses hyperedge structure; Group C to Group E (Parquet, functor instances) faces an instance-theory mismatch.
panproto’s IR is not a fixed data structure but a schema graph whose shape is determined by extensible theories. This distinguishes it from pandoc’s fixed document AST: new schema formats extend the theory space, and the IR adapts accordingly.
Format conversion in panproto is not a separate subsystem. It is the composition of two operations—parse and emit—applied to the same Schema graph that the rest of the toolchain already uses for diffs, migrations, and lens derivation.

title: 13  Schematic Version Control
text:
13.1 The Git Analogy
Schemas evolve. Fields get added, types change, constraints tighten. When multiple teams develop against the same schema, their changes diverge and eventually need merging. The schema CLI provides git-style version control for this process, except that instead of tracking text it tracks mathematical structure.
The important difference: git merges text and hopes the result compiles. panproto merges structure and guarantees data integrity.

text:
Git concept

Schema (graph structure)


Diff/patch

Content-addressing (blake3)

title: 13  Schematic Version Control
text:
13.2 Quick Start
# Initialize a schema repository
schema init

# Stage and commit a schema
schema add schema.json
schema commit -m "initial ATProto schema"

# Create a branch for a feature
schema branch add-verification
schema checkout add-verification

# Evolve the schema
schema add schema-v2.json
schema commit -m "add verification status field"

# Merge back
schema checkout main
schema merge add-verification

title: 13  Schematic Version Control
text:
13.3 The Object Store
Every schema, migration, and commit is identified by a blake3 hash of its canonical form. Two schemas with the same structure produce the same hash, even if they were created independently by different teams on different continents. Identity comes from structure, not naming.
.panproto/
  objects/<hex[0..2]>/<hex[2..]>   # content-addressed objects
  refs/heads/main                   # branch pointers
  refs/tags/v1.0                    # tag pointers
  HEAD                              # current branch
  logs/                             # reflog (audit trail)

title: 13  Schematic Version Control
text:
13.4 Structural Merge
When two branches evolve a schema independently, panproto computes a three-way merge:

Find the merge base (lowest common ancestor in the DAG)
Compute diff(base, ours) and diff(base, theirs)
Apply all non-conflicting changes from both sides
Flag overlapping modifications as conflicts

Conflicts are structural, not textual. A BothModifiedVertex conflict means both branches changed the same vertex’s kind in incompatible ways. A RemovedVariant conflict means a coproduct variant was removed, which would make existing tagged data ill-typed. These are things a textual merge cannot detect.

title: 13  Schematic Version Control
text:
13.5 Lifting Data Through History
The lift command migrates actual instance data through any path in the version DAG:
# Migrate a record from v1 to v3
schema lift record.json v1.0 v3.0
This composes the migrations along the path v1 → v2 → v3 into a single migration, then applies the functorial lift. The commutativity guarantee ensures you get the same result regardless of which path you take through the DAG.

title: 14  The Building-Block Landscape
text:
14.1 The Structure-Validation Boundary
panproto’s 76 protocol definitions are composed from 27 building-block theories via colimit. Each building block captures a structural distinction that affects migration correctness: whether collections are ordered, whether types form coproducts, whether references are recursive, whether edges are linear. This chapter catalogs all 27 and explains when each is needed.
Some schema properties are algebraically natural, expressible as GAT sorts, operations, and equations. Others are irreducibly computational: cardinality bounds, regex patterns, format constraints. The distinction matters because algebraic properties compose cleanly through colimits and migrations, while validation properties must be checked imperatively.
Structural (expressible in GATs):
Validation (requires ThConstraint):
The mechanism for lifting properties into structure is Cartmell’s property-to-structure move (Cartmell 1986). A conditional property like injectivity (“if f(a) = f(b) then a = b”) cannot be stated as an unconditional GAT equation. But you can add a retraction operation r with the unconditional equation r(f(a)) = a, which encodes injectivity as algebraic structure rather than as a logical property. This is why GATs have dependent sorts.

title: 14  The Building-Block Landscape
text:
14.6 Equations as Structural Invariants
Only 6 of the 27 theories have equations. Each has been type-checked against the theory’s sort declarations.
ThRecursion: unfold(fold(v)) = v
fold(v) has sort Mu (since fold: Vertex → Mu). unfold(fold(v)) has sort Vertex (since unfold: Mu → Vertex). The right side v also has sort Vertex. Both sides match.
ThReflexiveGraph: src(id(v)) = v and tgt(id(v)) = v
id(v) has sort Edge (since id: Vertex → Edge). src(id(v)) has sort Vertex (since src: Edge → Vertex). The right side v also has sort Vertex. Both sides match. Same reasoning for tgt.
ThSymmetricGraph: three equations
src(inv(e)) = tgt(e): both sides have sort Vertex. tgt(inv(e)) = src(e): both sides have sort Vertex. inv(inv(e)) = e: both sides have sort Edge.
ThCoproduct: variant_of(injection(v)) = v
injection(v) has sort Vertex (since injection: Variant → Vertex). variant_of(injection(v)) has sort Variant (since variant_of: Vertex → Variant). The right side v also has sort Variant. Both sides match.
ThPartial: defined(witness(d)) = d
witness(d) has sort Vertex (since witness: Defined → Vertex). defined(witness(d)) has sort Defined (since defined: Vertex → Defined). The right side d also has sort Defined. Both sides match.






Cartmell, John. 1986. “Generalised Algebraic Theories and Contextual Categories.” Annals of Pure and Applied Logic 32: 209–43. https://doi.org/10.1016/0168-0072(86)90053-9.

title: 15  Category Theory Cheatsheet
text:
15.1 Category
A quick reference for the categorical concepts that appear in this tutorial. Each entry gives a one-sentence definition, where it shows up in panproto, and a pointer to the literature.
A collection of objects and morphisms (arrows between objects) equipped with composition and identities, satisfying associativity and unit laws.
In panproto: The category GAT has theories as objects and theory morphisms as arrows. The category Mod(T) has models of a theory \(T\) as objects and model homomorphisms as arrows. See (Mac Lane 1998, Ch. 1).

title: 15  Category Theory Cheatsheet
text:
15.2 Functor
A structure-preserving map between categories: it sends objects to objects and morphisms to morphisms, preserving composition and identities.
In panproto: A theory morphism \(F: T_1 \to T_2\) induces a functor \(F^*: \mathbf{Mod}(T_2) \to \mathbf{Mod}(T_1)\) that pulls back models. This is the restrict operation—data migration in the reverse direction. See (Mac Lane 1998, Ch. 1).

title: 15  Category Theory Cheatsheet
text:
15.3 Natural Transformation
A family of morphisms between two functors that commutes with the functors’ action on morphisms.
In panproto: Natural transformations between migration functors correspond to systematic ways of transforming one migration strategy into another. They ensure that different migration paths yield compatible results. See (Mac Lane 1998, Ch. 2).

title: 15  Category Theory Cheatsheet
text:
15.4 Limit
A universal cone over a diagram—the “most general” object that maps into every object in the diagram compatibly.
In panproto: Products (a special case of limits) appear when combining independent schemas. The limit construction ensures no information is lost. See (Mac Lane 1998, Ch. 5).

title: 15  Category Theory Cheatsheet
text:
15.5 Colimit
A universal cocone under a diagram—the “most general” object that every object in the diagram maps into, gluing shared parts together.
In panproto: Colimits of theories are how new protocols are defined. The pushout (a colimit of a span) merges two theories along a shared base. See (Mac Lane 1998, Ch. 5) and (Adámek and Rosický 1994, Ch. 11).

title: 15  Category Theory Cheatsheet
text:
15.6 Pushout
The colimit of a span \(A \leftarrow S \rightarrow B\): the universal object that glues \(A\) and \(B\) together along their shared sub-object \(S\).
In panproto: colimit(t1, t2, shared) computes a pushout of theories. This is the primary mechanism for composing component theories into protocols. See (Barr and Wells 1990, Ch. 9).

title: 15  Category Theory Cheatsheet
text:
15.7 Adjunction
A pair of functors \(F \dashv G\) between two categories such that \(\mathrm{Hom}(FA, B) \cong \mathrm{Hom}(A, GB)\) naturally. The left adjoint \(F\) freely constructs; the right adjoint \(G\) forgets structure.
In panproto: The migration functors \(\Sigma_F \dashv \Delta_F \dashv \Pi_F\) form adjoint triples. \(\Delta_F\) (restrict) is the pullback; \(\Sigma_F\) (left push) and \(\Pi_F\) (right push) are its adjoints. These adjunctions guarantee the round-trip properties of lenses. See (Spivak 2012) and (Mac Lane 1998, Ch. 4).

title: 15  Category Theory Cheatsheet
text:
15.8 Kan Extension
The universal way to extend a functor along another functor. Left and right Kan extensions generalize colimits and limits, respectively.
In panproto: The left and right pushforward operations (\(\Sigma_F\) and \(\Pi_F\)) are computed as left and right Kan extensions along the theory morphism \(F\). See (Mac Lane 1998, Ch. 10) and (Spivak 2012).

title: 15  Category Theory Cheatsheet
text:
15.9 Fibration
A functor \(p: \mathcal{E} \to \mathcal{B}\) with a lifting property: for every morphism in the base and object in the fiber above its codomain, there is a canonical “cartesian” lift.
In panproto: The projection from the category of all schemas (across all protocols) down to the category of theories is a fibration. Lifting along this fibration is what lets panproto migrate schemas when the underlying protocol theory changes. See (Barr and Wells 1990, Ch. 12).

title: 15  Category Theory Cheatsheet
text:
15.10 Initial Algebra
The least fixed point of an endofunctor: an algebra \(F(A) \to A\) such that every other \(F\)-algebra receives a unique homomorphism from it.
In panproto: W-types are initial algebras of polynomial functors. Recursive data structures (trees, nested records, lists) are modeled as W-types, and their migrations are derived from the universal property of initiality. See (Adámek and Rosický 1994, Ch. 10) and (Gambino and Hyland 2004).

title: 15  Category Theory Cheatsheet
text:
15.11 Polynomial Functor
An endofunctor built from sums, products, and exponentiation by a fixed set: \(P(X) = \sum_{a:A} X^{B(a)}\). Polynomial functors classify branching structures.
In panproto: Each schema determines a polynomial functor whose initial algebra is the type of well-founded data conforming to that schema. Migrations between schemas lift to maps between the corresponding polynomial functors. See (Gambino and Hyland 2004) and (Abbott et al. 2005).

A generalized algebraic theory: sorts, operations, equations.
